denmark.meta_line: DENMARK. Noble, 1496. Malmö or Copenhagen Mint. Hans. NGC AU-55.  Fr-3; Galster-24; Sieg-12;

hands with only 2 in museums, the National Museum of Denmark and the Hermitage in St. Petersburg; a 
third, unconfirmed example in a European museum is rumored to exist.
The jewel in the crown of any collection of Scandinavian numismatics, being one of the oldest Scandinavian 
gold coins, the first dated Scandinavian coin (not counting the mysterious Danish 1234 Penny) and one of the

denmark.meta_line: DENMARK. Goldgulden (Rhinsk Gulden), ND (ca. 1496-1497). Malmö or Copenhagen Mint. Hans.
denmark.body_excerpt:
DENMARK. Goldgulden (Rhinsk Gulden), ND (ca. 1496-1497). Malmö or Copenhagen Mint. Hans. 
NGC EF-45. Fr-4; Galster-27A; Sieg-10; Schou 3; Bruun-3840. Weight: 3.17 gms. Mintmaster: Herman 
van Nassau or Hans Sever. A wonderful example of one of Denmark’s first gold coins, struck to finance the 
German mercenaries in the war against the Swedish rebel king, Sten Gustafsson Sture. Displaying a handsome 
retained brilliance, with only light instances of actual circulation.  
The coin was formerly in the collection of the legal councillor Frederik Christian Bech (1817-1905) who acted as 
a judge in

denmark.meta_line: DENMARK. Silver Gulden, 1523. Malmö Mint. Christian II. NGC VF-35.  Dav-8220; Galster-38; Sieg-
denmark.body_excerpt:
DENMARK. Silver Gulden, 1523. Malmö Mint. Christian II. NGC VF-35.  Dav-8220; Galster-38; Sieg-
9; Schou-3; Bruun-3933. Weight: 29.49 gms. Mintmaster: Jørgen Kock. An unusually nice example of this 
important Silver Gulden for which the design was copied from the spectacular gold Noble of king Hans. Kock 
was appointed mayor of Malmö in 1523 partly for having assisted king Christian in financing his wars against 
Sweden by issuing debased and clipped coins. The present type is, however, struck in fine silver, presumably 
for the king’s personal use during his travels in the Netherlands and Ger

denmark.meta_line: DENMARK. Goldgulden (Rhinsk Gulden), 1527. Malmö Mint. Frederik I. NGC AU Details—Cleaned.
denmark.body_excerpt:
DENMARK. Goldgulden (Rhinsk Gulden), 1527. Malmö Mint. Frederik I. NGC AU Details—Cleaned.  
Fr-10; Galster-59; Jensen & Skjoldager-T21/25; Sieg-35; Schou-1; Bruun-4056. Weight: 3.28 gms. Mintmaster: 
Jørgen Kock. A very nice example of an excessively rare early gold type that is never seen on the market. A 
faint reddish hue adorns the obverse, promoting the delicate yet clearly defined details. Well-centered and 
struck on a clean planchet.
Whereas the obverse closely copies a design from his predecessor, the four shields on the reverse surrounding the 
national coat of arms with the three l

denmark.meta_line: DENMARK. Joachimsdaler (Taler), 1537. Copenhagen Mint; mm: Clover. Christian III. NGC EF-40.
denmark.body_excerpt:
DENMARK. Joachimsdaler (Taler), 1537. Copenhagen Mint; mm: Clover. Christian III. NGC EF-40. 
Dav-8226; Galster-102; Sieg-19; Schou-8; Bruun-4276. Weight: 28.69 gms. Mintmaster: Reynold Junge. A 
exquisite example of this early Danish Taler struck the year after the Danish Reformation. The model was the 
silver coins minted in the German city of Joachimsthal by the counts of Schlick. A small planchet flaw is seen 
on the reverse as so often on this type, though this is minor and not exceedingly distracting.
€10,000-€12,000
From the L. E. Bruun Collection.
Ex: H. H. J. Lynge Collection (Copenha

denmark.body_excerpt:

16; Sieg-46; Schou-1; Bruun-4299. Weight: 28.49 gms. Mintmaster: Jørgen Degener. EXCESSIVELY RARE 
one-year type with a lovely iridescent toning and a most impressive pedigree. The portrait is clearly inspired 
by the works of the German artist Jacob Binck who was appointed painter to king Christian, and might in

denmark.body_excerpt:

denmark.meta_line: DENMARK. Goldgulden (Rhinsk Gulden) Klippe, 1563. Copenhagen Mint. Frederik II. NGC AU-55.
denmark.body_excerpt:
DENMARK. Goldgulden (Rhinsk Gulden) Klippe, 1563. Copenhagen Mint. Frederik II. NGC AU-55.  
Fr-19; Hede-3; Sieg-23; Schou-3; Bruun-4421. Weight: 3.23 gms. Mintmaster and engraver: Johan of Vilna. 
Of the original mintage of 2,259 examples, only a handful is still in existence, with about three in private 
hands. Exceptionally well-struck on a broad an even flan. 
Struck from gold provided by the king’s brother-in-law, August, Elector of Saxony, in order to finance the Danish 
war efforts in the Northern Seven Years’ War, 1563-1570.
€15,000-€18,000
From the L. E. Bruun Collection.

denmark.meta_line: DENMARK. Ducat Klippe, 1564. Copenhagen Mint. Frederik II. NGC Unc Details—Cleaned.  Fr-22;
denmark.body_excerpt:
DENMARK. Ducat Klippe, 1564. Copenhagen Mint. Frederik II. NGC Unc Details—Cleaned.  Fr-22; 
Hede-4; Sieg-28; Schou-1; Bruun-4486. Weight: 3.51 gms. Minted by Hans Köppelin and Lauritz Hammer. 
Mintage: 1,399. EXTREMELY RARE, nicely-centered and clearly struck, with only limited signs of the noted 
cleaning. This is the first proper Ducat struck in Denmark. Although Ducats continued to be struck on a 
regular basis until 1802, this is the only one bearing a denomination (DOGKATE). 
Presumably struck in the naval anchor forge on Bremerholm next to the royal castle. In 1619 the forge was turned

denmark.body_excerpt:
DENMARK. Speciedaler (Prinsens Daler), 1590. Christian IV . NGC EF-40.  Dav-8230; Hede-43; Sieg-
93; Schou-1; Bruun-4594. Weight: 29.04 gms. A choice, nicely toned and highly detailed example of the  
RARE so-called “prinsedaler” (Prince Taler), which was struck on application from Gyrvhild Fadersdatter 
and Peder Brahe. The 3/4 profile portrait of the only 13 year old king wearing a ruff is very unusual in Danish 
numismatics but seems to be copied after a portrait by Hans Knieper, still in the Royal collection at Rosenborg 
Castle in Copenhagen. The surfaces are yet attractive, with a steely

denmark.meta_line: DENMARK. 1/4 Portugaloser (2-1/2 Ducats), 1593. Haderslev Mint; mm: Crescent. Christian IV .
denmark.body_excerpt:
DENMARK. 1/4 Portugaloser (2-1/2 Ducats), 1593. Haderslev Mint; mm: Crescent. Christian IV . 
NGC AU Details—Bent. Fr-64; Hede-7B; Sieg-156.2; Schou 2; Harck-8.2; Bruun-4601. Weight: 8.75 gms. 
Mintmaster: Andreas Metzner. Only two examples known to exist, the present and the one in the National 
Museum of Denmark. Although the flan is slightly wavy, the overall condition is appealing with exquisite 
details and traces of original luster. 
This interesting and otherwise completely unobtainable issue was struck on the orders of the dowager queen 
Sophia on behalf of her son, who was only 16 at

denmark.meta_line: DENMARK. Goldgulden (Ungersk Gylden), 1593. Haderslev Mint; mm: Crescent. Christian IV . NGC
denmark.body_excerpt:
DENMARK. Goldgulden (Ungersk Gylden), 1593. Haderslev Mint; mm: Crescent. Christian IV . NGC 
AU Details—Cleaned. Fr-32; Hede-8B; Sieg-154.2; Schou-3; Harck-9; Bruun-4602. Weight: 3.45 gms.

The engraver copied the design of the Hungarian model too closely when he showed the elected king Christian

denmark.meta_line: DENMARK. Speciedaler, 1596. Copenhagen Mint. Christian IV . NGC AU-55+. Dav-8231; Hede-44; Sieg-
denmark.body_excerpt:
DENMARK. Speciedaler, 1596. Copenhagen Mint. Christian IV . NGC AU-55+. Dav-8231; Hede-44; Sieg-

denmark.body_excerpt:

denmark.body_excerpt:

144; Schou-1; Bruun-4773. Weight: 10.02 gms. Mintmaster: Hans Flemming. One of the most imposing from

on an even flan and is easily the best of the three known examples in private collections. Publicly owned 
examples are to be found in Copenhagen, Oslo, Stockholm, Lund and St. Petersburg.

denmark.body_excerpt:

48; KM-51; Hede-23A; Sieg-143.1; Schou-1; Bruun-4840. Weight: 8.91 gms. Mintmaster: Nicolaus Schwabe. 
A warm reddish toning along the rim further enhances this ravishing example. Only lightly circulated, with 
tremendous eye appeal as evidenced by the star designation NGC has bestowed on this example. 
The inspiration for this beautiful, amusing and  VERY RARE type came from the British Rose Nobles that 
were widely used in the international trade. In the contemporary official accounts the type is r

denmark.meta_line: DENMARK. 1/2 Rose Noble, 1611. Copenhagen Mint; Privy Mark: Clover. Christian IV . NGC MS-60. Fr-
denmark.body_excerpt:
DENMARK. 1/2 Rose Noble, 1611. Copenhagen Mint; Privy Mark: Clover. Christian IV . NGC MS-60. Fr-
49; KM-50.1; Hede-24A; Sieg-142.1; Schou 6; Bruun-4843. Weight: 4.38 gms. Mintmaster: Nicolaus Schwabe. 
A spectacular example of this  EXCESSIVELY RARE one year type boasting a typical early portrait of the 
king with a tall, open crown, as a closed crown was not used before 1624. No wear appears atop the designs,

legends.

denmark.body_excerpt:
DENMARK. 2 Krone, 1618. Elsinore Mint; Privy Mark: Crossed Clubs. Christian IV . NGC MS-61. KM-
61.1; Dav-3516; Hede-105B; Sieg-87.6; Schou-25; Bruun-5040. Weight: 37.85 gms. Mintmaster: Johan Post. A  
nicely toned and highly lustrous example, boldly struck with an insignificant die-break at the lower reverse.
This beautiful broad Krone (Corona Danica) is in many ways the archetypical coin of Christian IV . The 
denomination, inspired by the English Crown, was introduced in 1618 with the intention that it should pass 
for 1-½ Speciedaler but the king had arranged for the silver to be debased

Sieg-133.1; Schou 4; Bruun-5069. Weight: 2.95 gms. Mintmaster: Nicolaus Schwabe. Of the three registered 
examples this lovely piece is UNIQUE in private hands. The luster is bright and brilliant, with a generally 
sharp strike that only shows limited weakness. Entirely appealing and deserving of much attention from the 
numerous specialists seeking out an example of this type. 
When the new silver Krone was introduced in 1618 it was at the same time decided that an issue with the same 
value (1-1/2

denmark.meta_line: DENMARK. Gold 1/2 Krone, 1619. Copenhagen Mint; Privy Mark: Clover. Christian IV . NGC MS-63.
denmark.body_excerpt:
DENMARK. Gold 1/2 Krone, 1619. Copenhagen Mint; Privy Mark: Clover. Christian IV . NGC MS-63.

denmark.body_excerpt:

hovering crown is closely similar to the one used by Schwabe on the Mark issue from 1606 (Hede-92A). Both

denmark.meta_line: DENMARK. 2 Speciedaler, ND (1607-11). Elsinore Mint. Christian IV . NGC MS-62.  KM-147; Dav-
denmark.body_excerpt:
DENMARK. 2 Speciedaler, ND (1607-11). Elsinore Mint. Christian IV . NGC MS-62.  KM-147; Dav-
LS529; Hede-63D; Sieg-113.2; Schou-20; Bruun-5846. Weight: 56.36 gms. Mintmaster: Hans Flemming. A 
categorically sublime example, radiant and with meticulous details. This stunning presentation coin, inspired 
by the German Lösers, rather unusually shows the king wearing a feathered hat. 
The mint at Elsinore (Helsingør), the city of Shakespeare’s Hamlet, was only active from 1607 to 1611, in which 
period it was run by the Dutchman, Hans Flemming.
€10,000-€15,000
From the L. E. Bruun Collection.

The 3 Ducat  has consistently ranked among the most popular of all Danish coins and has for the last 100 
years been considered unattainable, but here it is in completely spectacular condition, superbly struck and 
with almost full original luster. For the denomination, the only other known example is in the National 
Museum of Denmark.

late middle-ages been a part of Denmark’s oldest and most

denmark.body_excerpt:

A charming example that is often mistakenly referred to as an “ Anointment Taler” on a

denmark.meta_line: DENMARK. Krone, 1680-GS. Copenhagen Mint. Christian V . NGC MS-62. KM-365; Hede-76; Sieg-36;

the fields, with a deep appearance as the peripheries are reached. Nice luster beneath attractive toning, this

because of the abounding luster. The type as such is excessively rare and this variant is in fact UNIQUE with

denmark.body_excerpt:

denmark.body_excerpt:

denmark.body_excerpt:

monarch was king by the grace of God (Dei Gratia).

between open field and design. Exceedingly alluring, as evidenced by the “star” modifier, highlighting 
exceptional eye appeal.
€8,000-€12,000
From the L. E. Bruun Collection.

DENMARK. Penny, ND (1018-1035). Viborg Mint. Cnut 
(Knud den Store). NGC MS-63. Hauberg-49; Hauberg Col-
lection-; Bruun-1340; Weight: 0.80 gms. A sensational example 
with a dazzling iridescent sheen and a remarkable strike. As op-
posed to the English types minted for Æthelred II, the Manus 
Dei (“Hand of God”) extending down from the sky is placed on 
the obverse. 
€1,500-€2,000
From the L. E. Bruun Collection.

denmark.meta_line: DENMARK. Penny, ND (1035-1042). Lund Mint. Harthacnut (Hardeknud). NGC Unc Details—Peck Marked.
denmark.body_excerpt:
DENMARK. Penny, ND (1035-1042). Lund Mint. Harthacnut (Hardeknud). NGC Unc Details—Peck Marked. 
Hauberg-2; Hauberg Collection-502; Malmer-14.801.1603; Bruun-1394; Weight: 0.85 gms. Moneyer: Alfward. A stun-
ning example with a razor sharp strike on a completely even flan. 
This excessively rare type is especially interesting because the obverse with the four-lined horizontal legend is one of the 
earliest examples of a Danish type being inspired by Byzantine issues such as the Miliaresia from the late 10th century. The 
reverse was also used in combination with the Nordic serpent type.
€4,000

denmark.meta_line: DENMARK. Penny, ND (1035-1042). Lund Mint. Harthacnut (Hardeknud). NGC AU Details—Peck Marked. Hau-
denmark.body_excerpt:

berg-4; Hauberg Collection-; Malmer-14.A78-b; Bruun-1395; Weight: 1.48 gms. A superbly toned example of an ex -
tremely rare type imitating the Æthelred II’s “ Agnus Dei” type. 
The contemporary hole is a clear indication that this particular religious type with the Lamb of God and the Holy Dove held 
a very special position and was not considered a mere coin but rather an amulet.
€3,000-€4,000
From the L. E. Bruun Collection.

thacnut (Hardeknud). NGC Unc Details—Peck Marked. Hau-
berg-38; Hauberg Collection-567; Bruun-1477; Weight: 1.00 
gms. Moneyer: Ulf. Extremely rare, well-preserved and highly

LE(X) LU(X) PA(X) (“King Law Light Peace”) forms a cross 
within the circle of the obverse.

denmark.meta_line: DENMARK. Penny, ND (1035-1042). Aalborg Mint. Har -
denmark.body_excerpt:
DENMARK. Penny, ND (1035-1042). Aalborg Mint. Har -
thacnut (Hardeknud). NGC Unc Details—Peck Marked. Hau-
berg-39; Hauberg Collection-570; Bruun-1478; Weight: 0.73 
gms. Moneyer: Alfric. An utterly captivating example of this ex-
ceptionally rare and important legible variant with the name of 
both the moneyer and the Mint on the reverse: +ALFRIC ON 
ALEBV .
This is a wonderful example of the potential importance of numis-
matic evidence as a written source, as it is the very first documen-
tation of the name of the city of Aalborg. 
€2,000-€3,000
From the L. E. Bruun Collection.

denmark.meta_line: DENMARK. Penny, ND (1042-1047). Haithabu (Hedeby) Mint. Magnus I “the Good” (Magnus den gode). NGC
denmark.body_excerpt:
DENMARK. Penny, ND (1042-1047). Haithabu (Hedeby) Mint. Magnus I “the Good” (Magnus den gode). NGC 
Unc Details—Bent. Hauberg-38; Hauberg Collection-727; C. J. Becker, NNUM, 1983, p. 42 ff.; Bruun-1632; Weight: 
0.74 gms. Moneyer: Iule. Apart from light blotches of verdigris, a remarkably lovely piece. Not only is this an extremely 
rare type, it is also highly unusual. The obverse design with the king being shown en face and the fortress on the reverse 
are quite unique, and the reverse legend where the moneyer lets the coin speak is equally remarkable: +IVLE ME FECIT 
(“Jule had me made”).
€

denmark.meta_line: DENMARK. Penny, ND (1047-1075). Lund Mint. Svend Estridsen. NGC Unc Details—Peck Marked. Hauberg-16;
denmark.body_excerpt:
DENMARK. Penny, ND (1047-1075). Lund Mint. Svend Estridsen. NGC Unc Details—Peck Marked. Hauberg-16; 
Hauberg Collection-910; Bruun-1712; Weight: 0.89 gms. Moneyer: Wulho. Excellent details and considerable traces of 
luster remaining.
The coinage of Svend Estridsen is strongly influenced by the Byzantine coin types that were partly brought back home by 
Varangians who served as personal bodyguards to the emperor in Constantinople. The present type is derived from the 
Solidi struck by Constantine VII Porpyrogenitus (913 - 959) showing the emperor being crowned by Christ. Although not 
mention

religious type featuring the Lamb of God (nimbate) and the Holy Dove in an interesting style with the Dove resembling 
a Roman legionary eagle.

denmark.meta_line: DENMARK. Penny, ND (1047-1075). Lund Mint. Svend Es-
denmark.body_excerpt:
DENMARK. Penny, ND (1047-1075). Lund Mint. Svend Es-
tridsen. NGC Unc Details—Peck Marked. Hauberg-26; Hau-
berg Collection-; Bruun-1798; Weight: 0.76 gms. Moneyer: Seg-
rim. A splendid example of this extremely rare type of which this 
is the only one to have been registered in private hands. 
This very important type connects the early Lund issues with the 
enthroned figure of Christ, Hauberg-6 (begun before the death of 
Magnus) with the late types featuring an elaborate short voided 
cross, Hauberg-28 (begun during the late 1050s). 
€3,000-€4,000
From the L. E. Bruun Collection.

denmark.body_excerpt:

gave the throne to her son, Frederik X, Eric III is the only king on 
the Danish throne to voluntarily

tainly overly conservative. Apart from a small area with a weak 
strike the present example is both sharply struck and retains 
a significant amount of luster. Excessively rare and interesting 
early type.
The fact that the crown of the king is nimbate means that the 
standing figure is not a representation of Christian II but rather 
a predecessor on the Danish throne, Canute IV (1080-1086) who 
was recognized by t

denmark.meta_line: DENMARK. 2 Skilling, 1533. Malmö Mint. NGC MS-62. Gal-
denmark.body_excerpt:
DENMARK. 2 Skilling, 1533. Malmö Mint. NGC MS-62. Gal-
ster-81; Sieg-1; Schou-2; Bruun-4153. Weight: 1.90 gms. A truly 
spectacular example of this highly interesting and excessively 
rare issue boasting a nice luster and exceptionally well-struck 
details.
Struck by the Council of State (SENATUS REGNI DANIE) be -
tween the death of Frederik I on April 10th 1533 and the outbreak 
of the so-called Count’s Feud (a war of succession) eventually lead-
ing to the victory of Christian III in 1536.  
€4,000-€5,000
From the L. E. Bruun Collection.
Ex: Frederik Chr. Bech collection (Copenhagen - 1906)

denmark.meta_line: DENMARK. 2 Mark Klippe, 1535. Aarhus Mint. Christian
denmark.body_excerpt:
DENMARK. 2 Mark Klippe, 1535. Aarhus Mint. Christian 
III. NGC AU-55. Galster-92B; Sieg-16; Schou-48; Bruun-4155. 
Weight: 11.05 gms. Mintmaster: Reynold Junge. Exceptionally 
well-struck. 
This extremely rare klippe was issued as payment for the merce -
nary troops hired by Christian (III) during the war of succession 
between Christian (III) and his cousin Christian II. It was struck 
in Aarhus, where the former mintmaster of Gottorp, Reynold Junge 
had established a new mint, as the elected king needed one within 
the kingdom itself. 
€5,000-€6,000
From the L. E. Bruun Collection.
Ex: H. H.

denmark.meta_line: DENMARK. 2 Mark, 1535. Roskilde Mint. Christian III. NGC
denmark.body_excerpt:
DENMARK. 2 Mark, 1535. Roskilde Mint. Christian III. NGC 
AU-58. Galster-97; Sieg-14; Schou-9; Bruun-4186. Weight: 14.96 
gms. Mintmaster: Reynold Junge. Although somewhat double-
struck, the present example is vastly superior to what is normally 
seen at auctions. An unusually nice portrait, struck on a sound 
flan with considerable luster remaining.
During the war of succession, the so-called Count’s Feud, the Mint 
had to move with the army, and in August 1535 Reynold Junge 
established a new Mint at the royal court in Roskilde. 
€2,000-€2,500
From the L. E. Bruun Collection.
Ex: Joh. G. Gu

denmark.meta_line: DENMARK. 8 Skilling (4 Skilling), 1535. Stockholm Mint.
denmark.body_excerpt:

Christian III. NGC AU Details—Cleaned. Galster-249; Sieg-
24; Schou-55; Delzanno-309; SM-259; Bruun-4232. Weight: 3.96 
gms. Imitative issue struck by the Swedish king Gustav Vasa in 
the name of Christian III. Lightly chipped at the outer rim but 
exceptionally well-struck and highly detailed.
According to T. G. Appelgren this extraordinarily rare issue was 
minted to pay the German mercenary soldiers in Scania who in -
sisted on being paid with Danish money.

From the L. E. Bruun Collection.

denmark.body_excerpt:

Schou-2; Bruun-4300. Weight: 28.69 gms. Mintmaster: Jørgen Degener. With just a hair-line split in the flan to detract 
from a formidable eye-appeal this is a wonderfully desirable example of this excessively rare one-year type. The portrait 
is clearly inspired by the works of the German artist Jacob Binck who was appointed painter to king Christian, and might 
in fact be his work. 
The Royal Flensburg mint was established in 1544 as the former mint in Gottorp had passed to the king’s

denmark.meta_line: DENMARK. 1/2 Silver Gulden, 1545. Flensburg Mint. Christian III. NGC AU Details—Tooled. Hede-17A; Sieg-
denmark.body_excerpt:

44.1; Schou-3; Bruun-4301. Weight: 14.50 gms. Mintmaster: Jørgen Degener. Although lightly tooled this can only be 
described as a phenomenal example, and arguably one of the most important coins in the whole collection. Only two 
examples of the Half Taler of this overall type are known, one in the National Museum of Copenhagen (holed) and the 
present unique and markedly different variant where the reverse shield is crowned.

denmark.meta_line: DENMARK. Gold Gulden (Ungersk Gylden) Klippe, 1563. Copenhagen Mint. Frederik II. NGC MS-61. Fr-21;
denmark.body_excerpt:
DENMARK. Gold Gulden (Ungersk Gylden) Klippe, 1563. Copenhagen Mint. Frederik II. NGC MS-61. Fr-21; 
Hede-1; Sieg-27; Schou-1; Bruun-4418. Weight: 3.76 gms. Mintmaster: Johan of Vilna (Hans de Willers). A lovely Mint 
State Klippe, with bright luster and limited handling.
This klippe was struck in a number of 15,504 specimens to finance expenses in connection with the sudden outbreak of the 
Northern Seven Years’ War. They where rather crudely minted on unevenly cut flans and it is extremely rare to find an ex-
ample this nicely centered and struck on a broad flan.
€6,000-€8,000
From the L. E.

denmark.meta_line: DENMARK. Speciedaler / 3 Mark, 1563. Copenhagen Mint. Frederik II. NGC EF Details—Reverse Graffiti. Dav-
denmark.body_excerpt:
DENMARK. Speciedaler / 3 Mark, 1563. Copenhagen Mint. Frederik II. NGC EF Details—Reverse Graffiti. Dav-
8228; Hede-8B; Sieg-21; Schou-5; Bruun-4422. Weight: 28.76 gms. Mintmaster: Poul Fechtel. A contemporary owner’s 
mark in the lower reverse field doesn’t detract from the present example being an exceptionally handsome piece. This 
one-year type that quite unusually bears the date in Roman numerals (LXIII) has one of the highest silver fineness 
(0.937) of all Danish coins minted since the early medieval period. 
€1,200-€1,500
From the L. E. Bruun Collection.

denmark.meta_line: DENMARK. Mark, 1563. Copenhagen Mint. Frederik II. NGC MS-64. Hede-9B; Sieg-15.2; Schou-77; Bruun-4443.
denmark.body_excerpt:
DENMARK. Mark, 1563. Copenhagen Mint. Frederik II. NGC MS-64. Hede-9B; Sieg-15.2; Schou-77; Bruun-4443. 
Weight: 9.57 gms. Mintmaster: Poul Fechtel. More than 100 different variations are known of this interesting issue as it 
was in fact minted with the same date between 1563 and 1576. Although the Mark of 1563 is still very common among 
collectors in ordinary condition, it is however a true rarity in a spectacular condition as the present. 
€1,000-€1,200
From the L. E. Bruun Collection.
Extremely Rare and Lustrous Klippe

denmark.meta_line: DENMARK. Gold Krone (Golt Krone) Klippe, 1564. Copenhagen Mint. Frederik II. NGC AU Details—Scratches.
denmark.body_excerpt:
DENMARK. Gold Krone (Golt Krone) Klippe, 1564. Copenhagen Mint. Frederik II. NGC AU Details—Scratches. 
Fr-23; Hede-5; Sieg-26; Schou-2; Bruun-4487. Weight:3.39 gms. Mintmaster: Hans Köppelin. Some light scratches but 
exceptionally well-struck and lustrous and excessively rare with only a few in private hands. 
This interesting klippe, struck during the Northern Seven Years War, was minted in the naval anchor forge on Bremerholm 
in only 600 specimens. The mintmaster, Hans Köppelin was transferred to Flensburg in 1566 where he acted until 1570.
€15,000-€20,000
From the L. E. Bruun Collection.

denmark.meta_line: DENMARK. Gold Gulden (Rhinsk Gylden) Klippe, 1564. Copenhagen Mint. Frederik II. NGC AU-58. Fr-24; Hede-
denmark.body_excerpt:
DENMARK. Gold Gulden (Rhinsk Gylden) Klippe, 1564. Copenhagen Mint. Frederik II. NGC AU-58. Fr-24; Hede-
6; Sieg-24; Schou-3; Bruun-4488. Weight: 3.13 gms. An alluring golden-orange toning further enhances the already 
sharply struck details. Originally minted by Hans Köppelin in only 995 examples and today of the utmost rarity.
Presumably struck in the naval anchor forge on Bremerholm next to the royal castle. In 1619 the forge was turned into Hol-
mens Church, which still stands today.
€18,000-€22,000
From the L. E. Bruun Collection.

denmark.meta_line: DENMARK. 2 Mark Klippe, 1564. Copenhagen Mint. Frederik ll. NGC AU-55. Hede-13; Sieg-20; Schou-4; Bru -
denmark.body_excerpt:

un-4489. 15.51 gms. A nicely centered and lustrous example of a very rare date. 
These primitive and base coins, which were minted during the Northern Seven Years’ War, were so badly regarded by the 
population that the king had to erect gallows around the country in order to threaten the death penalty if anyone refused to 
accept the king’s coin at face value. 
€2,000-€3,000
From the L. E. Bruun Collection.

inspiring example with traces of luster. The only other example in private hands was struck from a different set of dies. 
That example (from the Seck and Hede collections) also has traces of mounting. 
This numismatically and historically highly important and weighty gold type was struck on the orders of the dowager queen 
Sophia on behalf of her son, who was only 14 and had not y

etrates the warm toning on this truly grand example of a highly elusive one-year type. 
This is the largest from a set of 6 denominations with a very uniform design for the king’s coronation on 29 August 1596. As 
opposed to the minor coins (16, 8, 4, 2, 1 Skilling) which were used as largesse money, the Specietaler was probably struck

Bruun-4634. Weight: 7.07 gms. A sharply struck and nicely re-toned example of this very unusual type, believed to have 
been struck in celebration of the marriage between the king’s younger sister, Hedvig and the Duke-Elector of Saxony, 
Christian II on September 12th 1602. 
The present example seems to be the only example outside the National Museum of Denmark, which also owns a unique 
example in gold with the weight of 6 Ducats. Of particular heraldic interest is the fact that the cross

tive, nicely struck example with a shimmering luster and a lovely iridescent toning.
This beautiful and amusing type was according to tradition struck as largesse money to be used in connection with the cel-
ebration of the Hanseatic city of Hamburg swearing allegiance to the Danish king on 30 October 1603.

From the L. E. Bruun Collection.

denmark.meta_line: DENMARK. 8 Daler Klippe, 1604. Copenhagen Mint. Christian IV . NGC AU-55. Fr-44; KM-27; Hede-10; Sieg-
denmark.body_excerpt:

amounts of mint luster. Whereas the 6 and especially the 4 Dalers, albeit rare, sometimes appear on the market, the hefty 
8 Daler is normally considered completely unobtainable. 
Schwabe who had immigrated from Saxony was assigned to the Royal court in 1595 and became the first to produce struck 
medals in Denmark. In 1602 he was appointed mintmaster in Copenhagen where this wond

denmark.body_excerpt:

Schou-26; Aagaard-4.2; Bruun-5981. Weight: 6.75 gms. Mintmaster: Henrik Köhler. Engraver: Hans zum Busch. A most 
enchanting example of the rare and popular Double Ducat.
For the celebration of the king’s coronation on the 23rd of November a series of unusual coins were minted on square flans to 
be used as largesse money. Silver coins with a total value of 1000 Rigsdalers were minted in denominations of 1/4, 1/6 and 
1/12 Speciedaler to be thrown to the crowd in the streets of Copenhagen

and gorgeously toned.

denmark.meta_line: DENMARK. Ducat, 1662. Copenhagen Mint. Frederik III. NGC MS-62. Fr-101; KM-236; Hede-16B; Sieg-116.2;
denmark.body_excerpt:

Schou-11; Aagaard-31.1; Bruun-6388. Weight: 3.48 gms. Mintmaster: Johan Stichmann. Engraver: Henrik Köhler (ob-
verse), Johan Blum (reverse). Very rare in this exceptional condition with both details and luster being way above what 
is normally encountered.
Re-using an old Köhler-die for the obverse, Stichmann had these Ducats struck in a large town-house in Købmagergade 
in the center of Copenhagen. The house later became the home of the famous Danish-Norwegian author, Ludvig Holberg.

amusing obverse with the personification of the winds blowing from the clouds over a monogrammed pyramid,

Meybusch (A.MEIBUS.F .). Silver strike of this beautiful accession striking with almost proof-like surfaces. 
While listed as a coin in KM, Friedberg (in gold), Hede, and Sieg, Karsten Kold makes a compelling argument in favor of 
this being a medal or at least a medallic issue. He argues that the name of the engraver was only allowed on medals at the 
time, and on this specime

denmark.meta_line: DENMARK. 3 Ducats, ND (1699). Copenhagen Mint. Frederik IV . NGC AU-58. Fr-207; KM-463; Hede-4; Sieg-37;
denmark.body_excerpt:
DENMARK. 3 Ducats, ND (1699). Copenhagen Mint. Frederik IV . NGC AU-58. Fr-207; KM-463; Hede-4; Sieg-37; 
Schou-3; Kold-148b; Bruun-7290. Weight: 10.33 gms. Mintmaster: Christian Wineke. Engraver: Anton Meybusch. In 
spite of a minute edge nick, the abundant luster and the excellent details make this completely on a par with the only 
other example registered, the one in the National Museum of Denmark.
This hefty triple Ducat struck in commemoration of the royal succession on August 25th 1699 was struck with the same dies 
as those used for the regular one Ducat.

denmark.body_excerpt:

denmark.body_excerpt:

denmark.meta_line: DENMARK. Penny (Bracteate), ND (1146-57). Northern Jutland. Canute (Knud) V . NGC—Chipped. Hauberg-4;
denmark.body_excerpt:
DENMARK. Penny (Bracteate), ND (1146-57). Northern Jutland. Canute (Knud) V . NGC—Chipped. Hauberg-4; 
Hauberg Collection -; Poulsen-JY22; Bruun-2250. Weight: 0.18 gms. Lightly chipped. Remarkably detailed and with a 
lovely, dark patina. Extremely rare, early bracteate giving the full name and title of the king: +KANVT REX DAN. 
Following the death of Erik Lamb, Denmark was thrown into a civil war between three pretenders to the throne, all descend-
ing from different Danish kings: Canute, the grandson of Niels, Svend, the son of Erik Emune, and Valdemar, the grandson 
of Erik Ejegod. 
€2,500

un-2274 (illustrated plate 10). Weight: 0.85 gms. The so-called

denmark.meta_line: DENMARK. Penny, ND (1157-82). Roskilde Mint. Valdemar
denmark.body_excerpt:
DENMARK. Penny, ND (1157-82). Roskilde Mint. Valdemar 
I (Valdemar d. Store). NGC AU-55. Hauberg-7; Hauberg Col-
lection-1465; Poulsen-SJ15; Bruun-2275 (illustrated plate 10). 
Weight: 0.76 gms. Very nicely struck and with a fine, old cabinet 
toning.
An extremely rare type presumably struck around 1158-1160 for 
Bishop Absalon, the legendary “founder” of Copenhagen, who is 
here highly unusual depicted in profile. An equestrian statue of 
him, erected in 1901 (on the 700th anniversary of his death) still 
stands on Højbro Plads in Central Copenhagen; the archbishop is 
wearing a battle helmet

denmark.body_excerpt:

teresting type where not only the name and title of the king: 
+W ALDEMARVS REX D (Valdemar, king of Denmark) but also 
the name and title of the bishop is given in full: +NICOLAVS 
EPC (episcopus = bishop) clearly demonstrating that the clergy 
had a substantial share in the right of coinage. Traces of double 
striking on the reverse.
Niels Stigsen of th

denmark.meta_line: DENMARK. Silver Gulden, 1518. Malmö Mint. Christian II. NGC VF-30. Dav-8220; Galster-38; Sieg-9; Schou-2;
denmark.body_excerpt:
DENMARK. Silver Gulden, 1518. Malmö Mint. Christian II. NGC VF-30. Dav-8220; Galster-38; Sieg-9; Schou-2; 
Bruun-3931. Weight: 28.88 gms. Mintmaster: Dines Blicher. An utterly captivating example with a lovely, old cabinet 
toning. Of the utmost rarity with only a handful of examples known. Struck from dies made for the Gold Noble (Fr-6) 
which is today only known in the Danish National Museum.
The design was copied from the Nobles of king Hans but with a new reverse legend taken from the Liber Sapientiae 9:7: XPS 
IHS ELEGIT ME REGEM POPVLO SVO (“Christ Jesus chose me to govern his people”)

ridslev. The reverse bears the Latin inscription: DEO DVCE MILITES TRUMPHANT (‘With God’s leadership the troops will

Whereas the Danish Arms with the three lions have been chosen for the reverse just like the preceding Skillings from Christian

denmark.meta_line: DENMARK. Gotland. Skilling, 1525. Visby Mint. Christian II.
denmark.body_excerpt:
DENMARK. Gotland. Skilling, 1525. Visby Mint. Christian II. 
NGC MS-61. Galster-143; Sieg-12; Llt-3; Delzanno-79; Schou-2; 
Bruun-4114. Weight: 4.24 gms. Mintmaster: Hans Seyer. Excep-
tionally well-preserved with most of the original silvering intact. 
Rare Siege type struck for the admiral and governor of Gotland, 
Søren Norby during the war between Christian II and his uncle, 
Frederik I. 
Norby’s use of the Danish Coat of Arms with the three lions leaves 
no doubt that he saw the island of Gotland as an integrated part 
of Denmark. 
€1,000-€1,500
From the L. E. Bruun Collection.

denmark.meta_line: DENMARK. Hvid, 1535. Copenhagen Mint. Christian II.
denmark.body_excerpt:
DENMARK. Hvid, 1535. Copenhagen Mint. Christian II. 
NGC VF-30. Galster-87; Sieg-1; Schou-38; Bruun-4147. Weight: 
0.41 gms. Mintmaster: Jørgen Kock. Excessively rare and histori-
cally interesting one-year type struck for Christian II during the 
so-called Count’s Feud, a war of succession that followed the 
death of Frederik I. 
€2,500-€3,500
From the L. E. Bruun Collection.
Ex: Countship of Brahesminde Collection (Private Sale - 1922).
Ex: O. T. Thomsen Collection (Copenhagen - 1880) Lot # 602.

denmark.meta_line: DENMARK. 4 Skilling, ND (1535). Malmø or Copenhagen
denmark.body_excerpt:
DENMARK. 4 Skilling, ND (1535). Malmø or Copenhagen 
Mint. Christian II. NGC MS-62. Galster-91; Sieg-5; Schou-3; 
Bruun-4149. Weight: 3.56 gms. Mintmaster: Jørgen Kock. Struck 
in the name of the German Count, Christopher of Oldenburg 
(1504 - 1566) who gave name to the Count’s feud. Christopher 
sided with the former king, Christian II in the war against Chris-
tian (III) and acted as regent in the eastern Denmark between 
1534 and 1536. A very difficult type to obtain in any condition. 
€700-€1,000
From the L. E. Bruun Collection.
Ex: Countship of Brahesminde Collection (Private Sale - 1922)

denmark.meta_line: DENMARK. 8 Skilling (klippe), 1535. Aarhus Mint. Christian
denmark.body_excerpt:
DENMARK. 8 Skilling (klippe), 1535. Aarhus Mint. Christian 
III. NGC AU-55. Galster-93; Sieg-12; Schou-239; Bruun-4156. 
Weight: 2.72 gms. Mintmaster: Reynold Junge. Sublimely struck 
example, very rare thus.
This unusual klippe was issued as payment for the mercenary 
troops hired by Christian (III) during the war of succession be-
tween Christian (III) and his cousin Christian II. It was struck in 
Aarhus, where the former mintmaster of Gottorp, Reynold Junge 
had established a new mint, as the elected king needed one within 
the kingdom itself. 
€3,000-€4,000
From the L. E. Bruun Collection

denmark.meta_line: DENMARK. Mark, 1535. Roskilde Mint. Christian III.
denmark.body_excerpt:
DENMARK. Mark, 1535. Roskilde Mint. Christian III. 
NGC Unc Details—Obverse Scratched. Galster-98; Sieg-13; 
Schou-28; Bruun-4194. Weight: 8.07 gms. Mintmaster: Reynold 
Junge. Although somewhat double-struck, the present example 
is vastly superior to what is normally seen at auctions. An unusu-
ally nice portrait, struck on a sound flan with considerable luster 
remaining.
During the Count’s Feud, the Mint had to move with the army, and 
in August 1535 Reynold Junge established a new Mint at the royal 
court in Roskilde. 
€2,000-€3,000
From the L. E. Bruun Collection.

man city of Joachimsthal by the counts of Schlick.

denmark.meta_line: DENMARK. 3 Mark, 1560. Copenhagen Mint. Frederik II. NGC VF Details—Graffiti. Dav-8228; Hede-8A; Sieg-21;
denmark.body_excerpt:

Schou-1; Bruun-4402. Weight: 28.10 gms. Mintmaster: Poul Fechtel. Engraved initials in lower reverse field. With an 
original minting of only 16 examples this is almost a miraculous find, and it is today believed to be unique in private 
hands with the only other known example resting at the National Museum. 
The extremely low mintage was due to the fact that the king opposed the minting of Speciedalers as it was simply not profit-
able. The rather common 1563 Speciedaler (bearing the dat

denmark.body_excerpt:

Fechtel. Extremely rare type with the date on the obverse in a 
condition decidedly better than the only other example that has 
been on the market (from the Hede, Lerche and Bonde collec -
tions).
These primitive and base coins, which were minted during the 
Northern Seven Years’ War, were so badly regarded by the popula-
tion that the king had to erect gallows around the country in order 
to threaten the death penalty if a

denmark.meta_line: DENMARK. , 1572. Copenhagen Mint. Frederik II. NGC AU-
denmark.body_excerpt:

50. Dav-8229; Hede-17; Sieg-22; Schou-10; Aagaard & Märch -
er-72.1; Bruun-4525. Weight: 29.07 gms. Mintmaster: Hans 
Delhusen. Engraver: Cort Dellhusen. Very nice example struck 
on an unusually fine flan and boasting a charming, iridescent 
toning.
This beautiful type, which for the first time carries the three crowns 
of Sweden in the Danish royal arms, was struck from silver paid by 
the Swedish king to reacquire the fortress, Elfsborg, which had been 
conquered by the Danes during the Northern Seven Years War.

denmark.meta_line: DENMARK. Mark, 1582. Frederiksborg Mint. Frederik II. NGC VF Details—Tooled. Hede-23; Sieg-18;
denmark.body_excerpt:

Schou-2; Bruun-4534. Weight: 8.62 gms. Mintmaster: Poul Gulden. Engraver: Christopher Angerer. The pres-
ent example of this highly interesting type, which is presumed unique in private hands, was in the Royal Danish 
Collection in the mid 19th cent. before it was traded with Count Bille-Brahe. 
This type is from the first series struck at the newly opened Mint in the Frederiksborg Deer Garden northwest of 
Copenhagen. This was the first time roller dies were used in Denmark. 
€3,000-€4,000
From the

denmark.meta_line: DENMARK. Half Portugaloser (5 Ducats), 1593. Haderslev Mint. Christian IV . NGC AU Details—Mount Re -
denmark.body_excerpt:

moved. Fr-63; Hede-6; Sieg-160; Harck-2.7; Schou-1; Bruun-4600. Weight: 17.32 gms. Mintmaster: Andreas Metzner. 
Exceptionally well-struck on an even flan. With an outstanding issue as the present it is by no means surprising that 
there is evidence of a contemporary mounting positioned right above the royal coat of arms presumably to be used as 
a medallion by high ranking dignitaries. In fact, most of the surviving Haderslev gold issues have traces of mounting. 
According to the most recent

denmark.meta_line: DENMARK. Mark, 1596. Copenhagen Mint. Christian IV . NGC AU-53. Hede-69; Sieg-63; Harck-4.2; Schou-5; Bru-
denmark.body_excerpt:

un-4625. Weight: 9.57 gms. Mintmaster: Gert van Campen. Very few examples are known in private hands of which 
this is far and away the best.
This historically important one-year type was struck as largesse money for the coronation on 29 August 1596.
€5,000-€7,000
From the L. E. Bruun Collection.

very small denomination (1/12 Skilling) was minted. Not surprisingly, it is very hard to come by in a decent condition, 
let alone a first-rate piece as the present.

denmark.meta_line: DENMARK. 8 Solidi, 1603. Copenhagen Mint. Christian IV . NGC MS-63. KM-15; Hede-74; Sieg-52; Harck-6.23.2;
denmark.body_excerpt:
DENMARK. 8 Solidi, 1603. Copenhagen Mint. Christian IV . NGC MS-63. KM-15; Hede-74; Sieg-52; Harck-6.23.2; 
Schou-8; Bruun-4650. Weight: 3.20 gms. Mintmaster: Nicolaus Schwabe. Engraver: Nicolaus Schwabe. A truly magnifi-
cent example with normally lost details and a blazing luster beneath an alluring iridescent toning. Scarce variant without 
curlicue below the elephant.
This beautiful and amusing type was according to tradition struck as largesse money to be used in connection with the cel-
ebration of the Hanseatic city of Hamburg swearing allegiance to the Danish king on 30 October 1603.

denmark.meta_line: DENMARK. 6 Daler Klippe, 1604. Copenhagen Mint. Christian IV . NGC AU-53. Fr-45; KM-26; Hede-11; Sieg-147;
denmark.body_excerpt:
DENMARK. 6 Daler Klippe, 1604. Copenhagen Mint. Christian IV . NGC AU-53. Fr-45; KM-26; Hede-11; Sieg-147; 
Schou-3; Bruun-4668. Weight: 13.05 gms. Mintmaster: Nicolaus Schwabe. A very nice example with all four corners 
sharply struck. Of the originally 143 examples minted few still exist.
Two different obverse dies were used with markedly different portraits of which this, resembling the one used for minor 
coins like the Soesling, Hede-82, is by far the rarest. At the time the value of 6 Daler corresponded to 3.5 Ducats (Hungarian 
Guldens).
€25,000-€35,000
From the L. E. Bruun Collection.

denmark.meta_line: DENMARK. Mark, 1606. Copenhagen Mint. Christian IV .

Aagaard-2; Harck-6.32.1; Schou-1; Bruun-4705. Mintmaster: 
Nicolaus Schwabe. Minted from roller dies. An excellent portrait 
and sublime details combine to make this an absolutely amazing 
example of the characteristic type with the “hovering” crown. 
€700-€900
From the L. E. Bruun Collection.
Ex: Johan Andreas Ferdinand Wolff (Copenhagen - 1882) Lot # 433.

denmark.body_excerpt:

denmark.body_excerpt:
DENMARK. Rose Noble, 1612. Copenhagen Mint; privy mark: clover. Christian IV . NGC MS-62. Fr-48; KM-51; 
Hede-23A; Sieg-143.1; Schou-2; Bruun-4865. Weight: 8.93 gms. Mintmaster: Nicolaus Schwabe. Delicate purplish hues 
further enhances the incredible eye-appeal of this magnificent piece boasting a splendid luster and wonderful details. 
The inspiration for this beautiful, amusing and very rare type came from the British Rose Nobles that were widely used 
in international trade. In the contemporary official accounts the type is referred to as a “Guldridder” from the Dutch, 
“Gouden rijder” .

shields is very rare.

minor obverse surface marks aside, this is an absolutely gorgeous example with a choice striking and a remarkable lus-
ter. The present specimen of this beautiful Taler type is with all likelihood unique as no other examples neither publicly 
held or in private collections have been recorded. 
The issue should probably be considered a pattern that was realized as the crown on the rev

denmark.body_excerpt:

trous example and decidedly better than the only other one known in private hands (Count Lerche Collection, 1992, lot

denmark.meta_line: DENMARK. 2 Gold Krone, 1648. Copenhagen Mint. Chris -

denmark.body_excerpt:

han Stichmann. Engraver: Henrik Köhler (obverse), Johan Blum

enchanting eye appeal.
Re-using an old Köhler-die for the obverse, Stichmann had these 
Ducats struck in a large town-house in Købmagergade in the cen-
ter of Copenhagen. The house later became the home of the famous 
Danish-Norwegian author, Ludvig Holberg.

denmark.meta_line: DENMARK. 1/2 Speciedaler, ND (1669). Copenhagen Mint. Frederik III. NGC AU-55. KM-Pn19; Hede-36; Sieg-
denmark.body_excerpt:
DENMARK. 1/2 Speciedaler, ND (1669). Copenhagen Mint. Frederik III. NGC AU-55. KM-Pn19; Hede-36; Sieg-
141; Schou-19; Aagaard-63.1; Bruun-6746. Weight: 14.50 gms. Mintmaster: Gotfred Krüger. Fine old cabinet toning. 
Extremely rare and enigmatic issue. It was struck with dies made for the 10 Ducat 1669 and might (together with the 
very few other examples of different weights, Schou-19a) be considered an off-strike in silver. The weight of the present 
singular example (Schou-19) is however consistent with what would be expected from a Half Taler and is by KM listed 
as a pattern.

denmark.meta_line: DENMARK. 8 Skilling (klippe), ND (1677-1678). Kristians -
denmark.body_excerpt:
DENMARK. 8 Skilling (klippe), ND (1677-1678). Kristians -
tad. Christian V . NGC MS-64 Brown. KM-8; Hede-133A; Sieg-
149.1; Schou-52; Aagaard-8S-1; Bruun-7277. Weight: 16.84 gms. 
Mintmaster: Hans Wilhelm von Meerheim. Uniface emergency 
issue minted during the Swedish siege of Christianstad (Kris -
tianstad) Fortress during the Scanian War. Although these prim-
itive issues are not really rare, stunningly preserved examples as 
the present are practically never seen on the market. 
€1,000-€1,500
From the L. E. Bruun Collection.

denmark.meta_line: DENMARK. 4 Skilling, ND (1677-1678). Kristianstad (Siege
denmark.body_excerpt:
DENMARK. 4 Skilling, ND (1677-1678). Kristianstad (Siege 
coinage). Christian V . NGC MS-63. KM-cf. 7 (copper); Hede-
134B; Sieg-148.2; Schou-55; Aagaard-4S-1; Bruun-7280. Weight: 
14.48 gms. Mintmaster: Hans Wilhelm von Meerheim. Uniface 
emergency klippe struck in lead during the Swedish siege of 
Christianstad (Kristianstad) Fortress during the Scanian War. 
As lead is extremely susceptible to both physical and corrosive 
damage finding a survivor in the present superlative condition 
is ultra rare. 
€1,000-€1,500
From the L. E. Bruun Collection.

Hede-34B; Sieg-24.2; Schou-7; Bruun-7333. Weight: 28.45 gms. Mintmaster: Christian Wineke the Y ounger. Superbly 
struck and with a smart toning along the rim after an old cleaning. In total only 309 examples were struck of the 1704 
Speciedaler financed by Christian Wineke the younger himself. Of the present variant with a plain edge and struck with 
an obverse die for the “Travel Taler” (Sieg 23.1-2) only two examples seem to be known today, the one in the National 
Museum and this particu

denmark.meta_line: DENMARK. 6 Mark (Rejsedaler), 1704. Copenhagen Mint. Frederik IV . NGC AU-58. KM-cf. 479.2; Dav-1289; NMD-
denmark.body_excerpt:
DENMARK. 6 Mark (Rejsedaler), 1704. Copenhagen Mint. Frederik IV . NGC AU-58. KM-cf. 479.2; Dav-1289; NMD-
1B; Hede-38; Sieg-23.3; Schou-9; Bruun-7335. Weight: 26.79 gms. Mintmaster: Christian Wineke the Y ounger. This is the 
scarcest of the three variations which is very difficult to find as attractive as the present with its excellent, cabinet toning 
and pleasant luster.
Struck for the king’s travel to Norway which lasted from the 29th of May until the 5th of September 1704.
The reverse bears an inscription by the famous Danish hymn writer, Thomas Kingo: MOD, TROSKAB, DAPPERHED OG 
HVAD DE

denmark.meta_line: DENMARK. 2 Ducats, 1708. Copenhagen Mint. Frederik IV . NGC AU-55. Fr-211; KM-486; Hede-15; Sieg-44;
denmark.body_excerpt:
DENMARK. 2 Ducats, 1708. Copenhagen Mint. Frederik IV . NGC AU-55. Fr-211; KM-486; Hede-15; Sieg-44; 
Schou-2; Bruun-7353. Weight: 6.94 gms. Mintmaster: Christian Wineke the Y ounger. A wonderfully lustrous and pre-
cisely struck example of one of the great rarities among the coins of Frederik IV . This Double Ducat featuring an elegant 
reverse design with an alternating pattern of crowned arms and monograms was minted in both 1708 and 1709 but no 
other examples of any of the dates are known to be in private ownership today with only one example of each date in 
the Danish National Museum.
€

denmark.body_excerpt:

Schou-1; Bruun-7365. Weight: 6.93 gms. Mintmaster: Christian Wineke the Y ounger. A wonderfully detailed example 
with a most enchanting luster. While the equestrian single Ducat from 1711 appears from time to time, the present 
Double Ducat struck from the same dies is believed to be unique in private hands, with the example in the Danish Na-
tional Museum being the only other example known.
€15,000-€25,000
From the L. E. Bruun Collection.
Ex: Countship of Brahesminde Collection (Private Sale

Schou-2; Bruun-7602. Weight: 3.45 gms. Mintmaster: Christian Wineke the Y ounger. Engraver: Magnus Gustavus Ar-
bien (A). This scarce and stunning Ducat was struck to honor the succession of throne on August 6th 1746. As was 
customary the deceased king is wearing laurels, a tradition that continued on the Danish accession pieces through 1863.

denmark.body_excerpt:

Bruun-7609. Weight: 3.46 gms. Mintmaster: Ingeborg Maria Wineke. Engraver: Magnus Gustavus Arbien (A). Superla-
tively attractive with brilliant fields and satiny semi-cameo devices.
The elegantly composed reverse very unusually bears a legend in Danish instead of Latin, as “FRA CHRISTIANSBORG” 
means “From Christiansborg”. It is evidently referring to the reverse imagery where a intricately detailed ship is seen leaving 
the harbor facing the fortress Christiansborg on the coast of Guine

Friedrich Struensee, who was also the mentally ill king’s physician.

denmark.meta_line: DENMARK. 32 Skilling, 1775. Copenhagen Mint. Christian VII. NGC AU Details—Cleaned. KM-628; Hede-26A;
denmark.body_excerpt:
DENMARK. 32 Skilling, 1775. Copenhagen Mint. Christian VII. NGC AU Details—Cleaned. KM-628; Hede-26A; 
Sieg-14.1; Schou-3; FP-27.1; Bruun-7783. Weight: 12.25 gms. Mintmaster: Hans Schierven Knoph. Engraver: Johan 
Ephraim Bauert. This unusual denomination corresponding to 2 Marks is known in two variations, one where the king 
is looking right and one where he is facing left. Both are rare but the present excessively so with less than a handful in 
private hands. Highly lustrous and beautifuly struck (Holger Hede’s example was double-struck).
€10,000-€12,000
From the L. E. Bruun Collection.
Ac

denmark.meta_line: DENMARK. 1/3 Speciedaler, 1798. Copenhagen Mint. Christian VII. NGC Unc Details—Cleaned. KM-653; Hede-
denmark.body_excerpt:
DENMARK. 1/3 Speciedaler, 1798. Copenhagen Mint. Christian VII. NGC Unc Details—Cleaned. KM-653; Hede-
14; Sieg-21; FP-P1; Schou-3; Bruun-7938. Weight: 9.64 gms. Mintmaster: Hans Jacob Arnold Branth (HIAB). Engraver: 
Peter (Pietro) Leonard Gianelli (PG). Extremely attractive example with the superlative luster further enhancing the 
slightly mat devices. Highly interesting pattern struck in October 1798 as Gianelli’s first attempt as a coin engraver. It is 
only known in two examples (this and the one in the Danish National Museum) both of which have the same die flaw 
on the reverse and the

denmark.meta_line: DENMARK. Ducat, 1802. Copenhagen Mint. Christian VII. NGC MS-63. Fr-283; KM-650; Hede-2; Sieg-29; Schou-1;
denmark.body_excerpt:
DENMARK. Ducat, 1802. Copenhagen Mint. Christian VII. NGC MS-63. Fr-283; KM-650; Hede-2; Sieg-29; Schou-1; 
FP-47.4; Bruun-7974. Weight: 3.49 gms. Mintmaster: Michael Flor. Engraver: Georg Valentin Bauert. A remarkable ex-
ample with an excellent luster.
The so-called “Wildman Ducat” was the last Danish Ducat type produced with the 1802 being the very last date of issue. 
This type is the only Danish coin which gives the exact weight and fineness in the design, suiting it for international trade. 
€3,000-€4,000
From the L. E. Bruun Collection.

denmark.meta_line: DENMARK. Speciedaler, 1769. Rethwisch Mint. Christian
denmark.body_excerpt:
DENMARK. Speciedaler, 1769. Rethwisch Mint. Christian 
VII. NGC AU Details—Reverse Cleaned. Dav-1304; KM-603; 
Hede-6; Sieg-34; Schou-8; FP-R3; Bruun-10781. Weight: 28.88 
gms. Mintmaster: Hans Schierven Knoph. Engraver: Johan 
Ephraim Bauert. An immensely lustrous and choicely struck ex-
ample boasting a magnificent toning with a plethora of shades of 
blue. The emission of this type was very brief, as the king on the 
11th of March 1769 decided that the reverse type should be re -
placed with the Danish Arms of three lions. Only two dates were 
struck, 1768 and 1769 with the 1769 being exces

denmark.meta_line: DENMARK. Gold Bracteate, ND (500-550). Northern Jutland (?). Migration Period. Mackeprang-340; Bruun-1180
denmark.body_excerpt:
DENMARK. Gold Bracteate, ND (500-550). Northern Jutland (?). Migration Period. Mackeprang-340; Bruun-1180 
(illustrated page 47). Weight: 1.29 gms. Suspension loop removed. A daylight crack is noted, but both the impression 
and the overall condition is exquisite.
Since the foundation of numismatics as a proper science in Denmark, the Scandinavian gold bracteates of the Migration 
Period have been treated alongside coins (cf. e.g., Bartholin, Antiquities Danicæ, 1689; Museum Regium, 1696 and 1710; 
Beskrivelsen af 1791, Class I, Plate I). Historically, many of the most prominent collectors too

denmark.meta_line: DENMARK. Semi-Bracteate, ND (965-75). Harald Blåtand
denmark.body_excerpt:
DENMARK. Semi-Bracteate, ND (965-75). Harald Blåtand 
(Bluetooth). NGC MS-63. Hauberg-1; Malmer-KG 9a (C1b, Pl. 
10:18 / C1a, Pl. 21:10); Bruun-1207; Weight: 0.33 gms. Perfectly 
struck and with a seductive bluish patina this Choice exemplar 
is bound to attract considerable attention. No other examples of 
this interesting variant with an extra cross on the lower right on 
the “Dorestat-side” was known to Brita Malmer.
Harald Bluetooth, who is normally credited with being the first 
Danish king to adopt Christianity has incidentally given name to 
the short-range wireless program known as “Bl

denmark.meta_line: DENMARK. Semi-Bracetate, ND (958-85). Haithabu (Hede -
denmark.body_excerpt:
DENMARK. Semi-Bracetate, ND (958-85). Haithabu (Hede -
by) Mint. Harald Blåtand (Bluetooth). NGC MS-62. Hau -
berg-2; Malmer-KG 10a (A3e, Pl.29:8 / A2a, Pl.31:14); Bru -
un-1290 (illustrated plate 2); Weight: 0.29 gms. Tiny daylight 
strike-through. A truly exceptional near Choice Mint State sur-
vivor of a very interesting variant of which Brita Malmer had 
only recorded the present piece. 
€2,000-€3,000
From the L. E. Bruun Collection.

denmark.meta_line: DENMARK. Penny, ND (1035-42). Lund Mint. Harthac -
denmark.body_excerpt:
DENMARK. Penny, ND (1035-42). Lund Mint. Harthac -
nut (Hardeknud). NGC MS-62. Hauberg-9; Hauberg Collec-
tion-509 (electroplate copy); C. J. Becker-H 13/63; Bruun-1398; 
Weight: 0.83 gms. Moneyer: Carle. A spectacular piece with su-
perb details and an exceptional luster. Becker knew of only three 
examples, of which this is the only one in private ownership. The 
other two are in the Danish National Museum (defect) and in 
Lund.
Although the Short Cross type was completely dominant in Eng -
land during the last years of Cnut (1029-36) and the earliest reign 
of Harthacnut, it never became a

denmark.meta_line: DENMARK. Penny, ND (1044-46). Lund Mint. Civil War
denmark.body_excerpt:
DENMARK. Penny, ND (1044-46). Lund Mint. Civil War 
(stridsperioden). NGC MS-63. Hauberg-23 (Harthacnut); 
Hauberg Collection-683 (Magnus); C. J. Becker-MX 25/77; Bru-
un-1419; Weight: 1.05 gms. Moneyer: “Emcr” . Choice mintstate 
exemplar sharply struck on a completely even flan and with an 
utterly irresistible turquoise patina.
Struck during the troubled years of fighting for power between the 
Norwegian king Magnus and Sven, the nephew of Cnut the Great. 
€1,500-€2,000
From the L. E. Bruun Collection.
GEORG FRIEDERICH TIMM  
(1746-1829)  
Georg Friederich Timm (1746-1829) worked as a metal

denmark.meta_line: DENMARK. Penny, ND (1035-42). Ørbæk Mint. Cnut (Knud
denmark.body_excerpt:
DENMARK. Penny, ND (1035-42). Ørbæk Mint. Cnut (Knud 
den Store). NGC MS-64. Hauberg-54; Hauberg Collection-80; 
Bruun-1515 (Harthacnut). Weight: 0.73 gms. Moneyer: Un -
known (blundered legends). A nothing less than fabulous Near 
Gem of this interesting type with a an almost surrealistically dis-
soluted portrait facing right. 
€2,000-€3,000
From the L. E. Bruun Collection.

denmark.meta_line: DENMARK. Penny, ND (1035-42). Ribe Mint. Harthacnut
denmark.body_excerpt:
DENMARK. Penny, ND (1035-42). Ribe Mint. Harthacnut 
(Hardeknud). NGC MS-63. Hauberg-48; Hauberg Collec-
tion-604; Bruun-1530 (illustrated plate 5); Weight: 0.78 gms. A 
rare and Choice Penny with a lovely iridescent patina and a mar-
velous luster.
The unusual helmeted portrait is very similar to the Norwegian 
Pennies struck for Olaf the Holy, c. 1025 (NMH-4-5) 
€2,000-€3,000
From the L. E. Bruun Collection.
    
  
Photo Enlarged 2x

denmark.meta_line: DENMARK. Penny, ND (1035-42). Haithabu (Hedeby) Mint.
denmark.body_excerpt:

Harthacnut (Hardeknud). NGC MS-63. Hauberg-50; Hauberg 
Collection-616; Bruun-1538 (illustrated plate 5). Weight: 0.80 
gms. Moneyer: Unknown (blundered legends). 
Not only is this piece in a highly attractive Choice Mint State 
condition, it is also struck on an unusually heavy planchet and 
from a pair of dies which were cut with much more skill and 
precision that normally found on this type. 
€2,000-€3,000
From the L. E. Bruun Collection.
    
  
Photo Enlarged 2x

denmark.meta_line: DENMARK. Penny, ND (1042-47). Lund. Magnus I, “the
denmark.body_excerpt:
DENMARK. Penny, ND (1042-47). Lund. Magnus I, “the 
Good” (Magnus den gode). NGC MS-62. Hauberg-6; Hauberg 
Collection-685; C. J. Becker-M 8/175; Bruun-1601 (illustrated 
plate 6); Weight: 1.04 gms. Moneyer: Outhencar. Near Choice 
with excellent details and a pleasant bluish toning.
Whereas the obverse die, which is an unusually exact copy of Aeth-
elred’s Helmet type from 1003-1009, was also used in the produc-
tion of two different Magnus types (Hauberg 8 and 9), the reverse 
die is known from combinations with no less than five different 
obverse dies in the name of Harthacnut. 
€1,000-€1,

denmark.meta_line: DENMARK. Penny, ND (1042-47). Haithabu (Hedeby) Mint. Magnus I, “the Good” (Magnus den gode). NGC
denmark.body_excerpt:

Unc Details—Peck Marked. Hauberg-38; Hauberg Collection-728; C. J. Becker, NNUM, 1983, p. 42 ff.; Bruun-1631 
(illustrated Plate 6); Weight: 0.61 gms. Moneyer: Iule. Wonderfully toned and detailed with the peckmarks having not 
the slightest negative influence on the eye-appeal.
Whereas the names of both the moneyer and the mint are clearly spelled out (IOLI ON EITHEBI) which is in itself 
quite extraordinary for this mint, it is particularly the obverse legend that deserves attention. Here, Magn

denmark.meta_line: DENMARK. Penny, ND (1047-75). Lund Mint. Svend II Es -

DENMARK. Penny, ND (1047-75). Lund Mint. Svend II Es -
tridsen. NGC MS-64. Hauberg-6; Hauberg Collection-820; Bru-
un-1776; Weight: 1.02 gms. Moneyer: Wulfet. A delicately toned 
Near Gem Byzantine type Penny struck from a pair of dies of 
unusually high artistic merit. 
€1,500-€2,000
From the L. E. Bruun Collection.
Ex: Countship of Brahesminde Collection (Private Sale - 1922).

denmark.meta_line: DENMARK. Penny, ND (1047-75). Lund Mint. Svend II Es -
denmark.body_excerpt:
DENMARK. Penny, ND (1047-75). Lund Mint. Svend II Es -
tridsen. NGC MS-62. Hauberg-28; Hauberg Collection-992; 
Bruun-1845. Weight: 1.18 gms. Moneyer: Ulfcil. Highly detailed, 
near Choice and with a perfectly adorable iridescent toning.
This beautiful Byzantine type was struck in the later part of Svend’s 
reign after he had changed his name on the coins from the Nordic 
“Svein” to the Latin, Magnus probably to underline his Christian 
faith. Interestingly, in this scarce variant an E-punch has been used 
lying down has been used as the first letter in Magnus instead of a 
regular M punch. 
€

denmark.meta_line: DENMARK. Penny, ND (1047-75). Lund Mint. Svend II Es -
denmark.body_excerpt:
DENMARK. Penny, ND (1047-75). Lund Mint. Svend II Es -
tridsen. NGC MS-63. Hauberg-32a; Hauberg Collection-cf. 
1053; Moltke-307; Bruun-1869. Weight: 0.90 gms. Moneyer: 
Thorgotr. Lightly chipped but otherwise an extremely well-pre-
served Choice exemplar of one of the highly interesting types 
from the last years of Svend’s reign bearing a reverse legend writ-
ten entirely in runes. 
€4,000-€5,000
From the L. E. Bruun Collection.
Ex: Countship of Brahesminde Collection (Private Sale - 1922).

denmark.meta_line: DENMARK. Penny, ND (1047-75). Roskilde Mint. Svend II
denmark.body_excerpt:
DENMARK. Penny, ND (1047-75). Roskilde Mint. Svend II 
Estridsen. NGC MS-63. Hauberg-36; Hauberg Collection-1078; 
Bruun-1884; Weight: 1.09 gms. Wonderfully struck and hand -
somely toned Choice Penny from the royal residential city of 
Roskilde.
The round “dot” on the upper arm of the cross has traditionally 
been described as a die flaw but large die studies of the emissions 
in Roskilde have established that it should be considered some sort 
of control mark (Fornitz, 1995). 
€1,000-€1,500
From the L. E. Bruun Collection.

denmark.meta_line: DENMARK. Penny, ND (1047-75). Viborg Mint. Svend II
denmark.body_excerpt:
DENMARK. Penny, ND (1047-75). Viborg Mint. Svend II 
Estridsen. NGC MS-63. Hauberg-56; Hauberg Collection - (cf. 
1135); Bruun-1931; Weight: 0.71 gms. A superlative exemplar of 
this interesting Penny where the obverse legend seems to contain 
a reference to Denmark (DENNOR).
The small cross in front of the portrait wasn’t added until rather 
late in the production making this particular variant very rare. 
€1,500-€2,000
From the L. E. Bruun Collection.
    
  
Photo Enlarged 2x

denmark.meta_line: DENMARK. Penny, ND (1047-75). Ribe Mint. Svend II Es -
denmark.body_excerpt:
DENMARK. Penny, ND (1047-75). Ribe Mint. Svend II Es -
tridsen. NGC MS-62. Hauberg-68; Hauberg Collection-1173; 
Bruun-1957 (here wrongly pedigreed as Petersen 424). Weight: 
0.76 gms. Moneyer: Unknown (blundered legends). A rare vari-
ant, nicely struck and with a thoroughly pleasing dark patina.
The obverse legend “DIO III” could - with a bit of good will - be 
considered a religious contraction for the Divine Trinity. 
€1,500-€2,000
From the L. E. Bruun Collection.

denmark.meta_line: DENMARK. Penny, ND (1076-80). Lund Mint. Harald III
denmark.body_excerpt:
DENMARK. Penny, ND (1076-80). Lund Mint. Harald III 
Hén. NGC MS-63. Hauberg-1; Hauberg Collection-1205; 
Poulsen-SK1.1a; Bruun-1989. Weight: 0.97 gms. Moneyer: Thor-
got. A Choice mintstate piece with an amazing eye appeal. The 
standing figure carrying a crook is nimbate and could be inter -
preted as an anonymous canonized bishop but might just as well 
be an interpretation of Christ as “the good sheperd” (John 10:1-21). 
€2,500-€3,500
From the L. E. Bruun Collection.
    
  
Photo Enlarged 2x

denmark.body_excerpt:

IV (Knud d. Hellige). NGC MS-63. Hauberg-7; Hauberg Col-
lection - (cf. 1237); Poulsen-Sj1.2a; Ramus-10; Bruun-2021; 
Weight: 0.69 gms. Moneyer: Bosi. Scarce moneyer. An utterly 
charming example with sublime details and a delightful luster. 
€6,000-€8,000
From the L. E. Bruun Collection.
Ex: Countship of Brahesminde Collection (Private Sale - 1922).
 Ex: Christian Jürgensen Thomsen Collection (Copenhagen - 1876) Lot

CHRISTIAN JURGENSEN THOMSEN  
(1788-1865)  
Christian Jurgensen Thomsen (1788-1865) was an internation -
ally famo

denmark.meta_line: DENMARK. Penny, ND (1080-86). Slagelse Mint. Canute IV (Knud d. Hellige). NGC MS-63. Hauberg-9; Hauberg
denmark.body_excerpt:
DENMARK. Penny, ND (1080-86). Slagelse Mint. Canute IV (Knud d. Hellige). NGC MS-63. Hauberg-9; Hauberg 
Collection - (cf.1253); Ramus-36; Poulsen-SJ1.2d; Bruun-2038. Weight: 0.90 gms. Moneyer: Atsur. A strikingly lustrous 
and decidedly Choice piece of the amusing type with the king shown ready for battle.
As the first Dane to be canonized by the Pope, king Canute received the honor on April 19, 1100. His canonization by Pope 
Paschalis II followed his 1086 assassination by rebels in St. Alban’s Priory in Odense. 
€6,000-€8,000
From the L. E. Bruun Collection.

denmark.meta_line: DENMARK. Penny, ND (1103-34). Roskilde Mint. Niels. NGC MS-65. Hauberg-9; Hauberg Collection -; Poulsen-SJ9; Bruun-2153.
denmark.body_excerpt:
DENMARK. Penny, ND (1103-34). Roskilde Mint. Niels. NGC MS-65. Hauberg-9; Hauberg Collection -; Poulsen-SJ9; Bruun-2153. 
Weight: 0.71 gms. Moneyer: Lon. A perfectly outstanding Gem mintstate with a vibrant luster underneath a delicate toning with hues 
of blue and yellow.
Very interesting architectural type inspired by the German Denarii and struck by a rare moneyer in the later reign of king Niels. 
€2,500-€3,500
From the L. E. Bruun Collection.

denmark.meta_line: DENMARK. Penny, ND (1182-1202). Lund Mint. Canute VI.
denmark.body_excerpt:
DENMARK. Penny, ND (1182-1202). Lund Mint. Canute VI. 
NGC AU-53. Hauberg-1; Hauberg Collection-1526ff; Poulsen-
SK22; Bruun-2329. Weight: 0.86 gms. A very conservatively 
graded “Valdemarian” Penny in a wonderful condition towering 
above what you could reasonably expect.
The king is shown looking over some sort of fortified, brick wall, 
whereas the legendary Archbishop, Absalon, the “founder” of Co-
penhagen” is depicted on the reverse in an imposing church portal. 
€1,800-€2,200
From the L. E. Bruun Collection.

denmark.meta_line: DENMARK. Silver Gulden, 1523. Malmø Mint. Christian
denmark.body_excerpt:
DENMARK. Silver Gulden, 1523. Malmø Mint. Christian 
II. NGC EF-40. Dav-8220; Galster-38; Sieg-9; Schou-5; Bru -
un-3934. Weight: 28.74 gms. Mintmaster: Jørgen Kock. A most 
handsome piece with excellent details and a fine iridescent ton-
ing. Rare variant with the king’s name misspelled: CHRISTER-
NUS.
Jørgen Kock was appointed mayor of Malmö in 1523 partly for 
having assisted king Christian in financing his wars against Swe-
den by issuing debased and clipped coins. The present type is, how-
ever, struck in fine silver, presumably for the king’s personal use 
during his travels in the Nethe

denmark.meta_line: DENMARK. 4 Skilling, ND (1532). Malmø Mint. Frederik
denmark.body_excerpt:
DENMARK. 4 Skilling, ND (1532). Malmø Mint. Frederik 
I. NGC MS-61. Galster-60; Sieg-26; Schou-11; Bruun-4090; 
Weight: 3.72 gms. Mintmaster: Jørgen Kock. An outstanding 
mintstate exemplar nicely struck on a perfect flan of sound metal.
Scholars and collectors have argued about the denomination of this 
coin for centuries. Sieg and Galster call it an 8 Skilling, while Schou 
(and therefore Bruun) call it a 4 Skilling. The most recent study, by 
Jensen & Skjoldager, again leans towards 4 Skilling. 
€1,000-€1,200
From the L. E. Bruun Collection.

denmark.meta_line: DENMARK. Gotland. Skilling, 1524. Visby Mint. Christian
denmark.body_excerpt:
DENMARK. Gotland. Skilling, 1524. Visby Mint. Christian 
II. NGC MS-62. Galster-143; Sieg-12; Llt-2a; Delzanno-77; 
Schou-3; Bruun-4112; Weight: 4.38 gms. Mintmaster: Hans Sey-
er. Almost Choice exemplar with most of the original silvering 
intact. Rare siege type struck for the admiral and governor of 
Gotland, Søren Norby during the war between Christian II and 
his uncle, Frederik I. Norby’s use of the Danish Coat of Arms with 
the three lions leaves no doubt that he saw the island of Gotland as 
an integrated part of Denmark. 
€2,500-€3,500
From the L. E. Bruun Collection.

denmark.meta_line: DENMARK. Mark, 1544. Copenhagen Mint. Christian III.
denmark.body_excerpt:
DENMARK. Mark, 1544. Copenhagen Mint. Christian III. 
NGC AU-53. Hede-3B; Sieg-37.2; Schou-1; Bruun-4288. Weight: 
9.31 gms. Mintmaster: Poul Fechtel. An attractive blue ton -
ing adorns this nicely struck, scarce issue bearing the first date 
struck after the Marks were debased by c. 6 %. 
€2,500-€3,500
From the L. E. Bruun Collection.
Ex: Countship of Brahesminde Collection (Private Sale - 1922).

denmark.meta_line: DENMARK. Gold Gulden (Ungersk Gylden) Klippe, 1563. Copenhagen Mint. Frederik II. NGC MS-61. Fr-21; Hede-1; Sieg-27;
denmark.body_excerpt:
DENMARK. Gold Gulden (Ungersk Gylden) Klippe, 1563. Copenhagen Mint. Frederik II. NGC MS-61. Fr-21; Hede-1; Sieg-27; 
Schou-1; Bruun-4420. Weight: 3.46 gms. Mintmaster: Johan of Vilna (Hans de Willers). A lovely Mint State Klippe, with fine details 
for issue. Flan lamination.
This klippe was struck in a number of 15,504 specimens to finance expenses in connection with the sudden outbreak of the Northern Seven 
Years' War the same year. They where rather crudely minted on unevenly cut flans and it is extremely rare to find an example this nicely 
struck.
€5,000-€7,000
From the L. E. Bruun Coll

denmark.meta_line: DENMARK. Hvid, 1582. Frederiksborg Mint. Frederik II. NGC MS-63. Hede-29; Sieg-2; Schou-26; Bruun-4547. Mintmaster: Poul
denmark.body_excerpt:
DENMARK. Hvid, 1582. Frederiksborg Mint. Frederik II. NGC MS-63. Hede-29; Sieg-2; Schou-26; Bruun-4547. Mintmaster: Poul 
Gulden. Engraver: Christopher Angerer. An utterly charming and distinctly Choice piece of this little minor that was minted at the 
newly opened Mint in the Frederiksborg Deer Garden northwest of Copenhagen. This was the first time roller dies were used in Den-
mark. 
€700-€900
From the L. E. Bruun Collection.
    
  
Photo Enlarged 2x

denmark.meta_line: DENMARK. 2 Skilling, 1583. Frederiksborg Mint. Frederik II. NGC MS-64. Hede-27; Sieg-8; Schou-17; Bruun-4558. Mintmaster:

Poul Gulden. Engraver: Christopher Angerer. A wonderfully lustrous and close to Gem piece which slight curvature clearly indicates 
that it was minted with roller dies.

From the L. E. Bruun Collection.

denmark.body_excerpt:
DENMARK. 1/2 Portugaloser, 1592. Haderslev Mint. Christian IV . NGC AU Details—Plugged, Tooled. Fr-63; 
Hede-6; Sieg-160; Harck-2.7; Schou-6; Bruun-4597. Weight: 17.38 gms. Mintmaster: Andreas Metzner. As almost all 
known examples from the Haderslev series of 1/4, 1/2 and 1 Portugalosers, the present piece has been mounted to be 
worn, evidenced by a plug above the arms. Attractively detailed and with a unusually even planchet. Only three pieces 
are known of this internationally coveted type: this, the one in the Danish National Museum and another example in 
private ownership that has also

denmark.meta_line: DENMARK. 1/4 Portugaloser, 1592. Haderslev Mint. Christian IV . NGC AU Details—Mount Removed. Fr-64;
denmark.body_excerpt:
DENMARK. 1/4 Portugaloser, 1592. Haderslev Mint. Christian IV . NGC AU Details—Mount Removed. Fr-64; 
Hede-3; Sieg-155; Harck-2.4; Schou-7; Bruun-4598. Weight: 8.69 gms. Mintmaster: Andreas Metzner. Remains from 
a contemporary mounting positioned right above the royal coat of arms presumably to be used as a medallion by high 
ranking dignitaries. Magnificently detailed with even the profile of the man in the moon clearly visible on the mintmark.
Of the handful of known exemplars of this important one-year type, only one other is in private hands. This was ac-
quired by L. E. Bruun with his pu

denmark.meta_line: DENMARK. 2 Skilling, 1595. Copenhagen Mint. Christian IV . NGC MS-61. Hede-67; Sieg-28; Harck-3.1.2; Schou-12; Bruun-4613.
denmark.body_excerpt:
DENMARK. 2 Skilling, 1595. Copenhagen Mint. Christian IV . NGC MS-61. Hede-67; Sieg-28; Harck-3.1.2; Schou-12; Bruun-4613. 
Moneyer: Andreas Metzner. A truly exceptional survivor of this scarce type bearing the legend REX ELECTVS (King-elect) and no 
crown above the shield indicating that it was struck before the actual coronation on the 29th of August 1596 when the king had come 
of age. 
€800-€1,200
From the L. E. Bruun Collection.

denmark.meta_line: DENMARK. 4 Skilling, 1596. Copenhagen Mint. Christian IV . NGC MS-62. Hede-71; Sieg-38; Schou-8; Harck-4.4; Bruun-4627.
denmark.body_excerpt:
DENMARK. 4 Skilling, 1596. Copenhagen Mint. Christian IV . NGC MS-62. Hede-71; Sieg-38; Schou-8; Harck-4.4; Bruun-4627. 
Mintmaster: Gert van Campen. An absolutely stunning near Choice example of this rare and interesting one year type which was 
struck in connection with the coronation of the king on 29 August, possibly as a largesse money.

Bearing a symbolic reverse, the ship design on this type designates that the gold used to coin this piece was acquired from 
overseas. The Latin leg

Bruun-7653. Weight: 3.50 gms. Mintmaster: Peter Nicolai van Hauen (VH). Engraver: Magnus Gustavus Arbien. The technical grade 
alone should say it all, but when holding the piece in your hand you can almost feel the wind in your hair. A maritime Ducat from the 
very first tier.
Bearing a symbolic reverse, the ship design on this type designates that the gold used to coin this piece was acquired from overseas. The Latin 
legend, “DUCE PRUDENTIA, CONSTANTIA COMITE”, which has root

denmark.meta_line: DENMARK. 1/2 Skilling, 1755. Copenhagen Mint. Frederik V . NGC MS-65 Brown. KM-577.1; Hede-40A; Sieg-1.1; Schou-4; Bru-
denmark.body_excerpt:

un-7656. Mintmaster: Peter Nicolai Van Hauen (PNVH). Engraver: Georg Wilhelm Wahl. Flaming red luster hugs the design, with 
sharp details throughout.
1/2 Skilling 1751 and 1755 were issued at the request of the governors of the poor houses, as a denomination smaller than 1 Skilling was 
needed for the church collection plates. The coins were recalled and demonetized in 1775.

denmark.body_excerpt:

Bruun-7667. Weight: 3.48 gms. Mintmaster: Peter Nicolai van Hauen (VH); Engraver: Magnus Gustavus Arbien (A). 
An absolutely stunning Gem of this beautiful one-year type which is presumed to be the only Danish Ducat struck from 
gold mined in Norway. Excessively rare in such a wonderful condition.
Of the original mintage of 1,001 coins, 700 were immediately re-issued as Ducat Courants. With a gold content of c. 20% 
less, these were both more practical for circulation and more profitable

which - as opposed to practically all other Ducats - was actually 
struck for circulation, making it almost impossible to find in a

is bare but is here shown with a light drapery around his

Hede-13; Sieg-39; Schou-5; Bruun-8019. Mintmaster: Ole Varberg. Engraver: Johannes Conradsen. A pleasing proof 
with rich eye-appeal and handsome toning. With only two examples recorded (the other one being in the National 
Museum of Denmark), this is the first time this type has been on the market for almost 140 years.
In 1812 three denominations were issued in copper, 12, 3 and 1 Skilling, whereas the projected denominations of 6 and 4

fered in this sale. A darker multicolor toning hugs the devices on 
the obverse, less so on the reverse.

struck before the design and metal was changed in 1924, with 1916 
being the last year of minting. 1915 and 1916 reuse the obverse

denmark.meta_line: DENMARK. 5 Øre, 1919. Copenhagen Mint. Christian X.
denmark.body_excerpt:
DENMARK. 5 Øre, 1919. Copenhagen Mint. Christian X. 
NGC MS-67. KM-814.2a; Hede-15B; Sieg-3.3; Schou-4; Bru-
un-8490. Mintmaster: Hans Christian Nielsen (HCN). Engraver: 
Gunnar Jensen (GJ). NGC TOP POP for both type and date. 
Single-year subtype in zinc-clad iron with mintmaster’s initials 
HCN. A type easily susceptible to rust with this example in pris-
tine condition, as struck, and scarce so.
In 1918, as the price of copper had soared during World War One, 
it was decided to strike Øre coins in iron covered by a thin layer 
of Zinc, giving them the characteristic oxidized, black surfaces

Gunnar Jensen (GJ). NGC TOP POP - tied for type. Lustrous, 
pristine surface on this key date, which is one of the most cov -
eted Danish decimal coins.
Not many 10 Øre coins were struck in 1923 as a new series was 
underway. The same goes for the 5 øre 1923, while no 25 Øre was 
minted that year. 
€2,000-€3,000
From the L. E. Bruun Collection.

town of Stade. In 1712 the now dukedom was conquered by the Danes an

un-12270. Weight: 22.13 gms. Mintmaster: Andreas Hille. A rare coin with only very light wear; somewhat rusty dies but

The Duchy of Bremen & Verden was occupied by the Swedish forces in 1632 and officially ceded to Sweden on the Westpha-
lian Peace of 1648. It remained Swedish till 1720, when it was again ceded to the Elector of Hannover, Georg (George I of 
Great Britain) at the end of the Great Nordic War.
The mint was placed in Stade as the City of Bremen was not

Sieg-35; Schou-1; Bruun-8080. Engraver: Johannes Conradsen. Mint Master: Johann Friedrich Freund (FF). 
Far surpassing the five other specimens of this date yet certified by NGC, this near-Gem glows with brilliant 
luster and is sharply struck in the intricate details. Light friction likely withholds this example from a higher 
grade, though this slight touch of handing does little to diminish the overall appeal. The technical surface 
preservation give this piece the appearance of a prooflike exampl

finest graded example, by an appreciable amount, of any type of this date, reaching a lofty Gem level. The 
surfaces are softly glowing, with nice allure in the open fields, and crisp frosty devices. Signs of marking and 
handling are virtually nonexistent, leaving this as a elite example fo

schleswig_holstein.body_excerpt:

off the appearance of a proof or specimen striking. The surfaces are essentially unmarked, and the details are

burg (Rendsborg). 
Although Rendsburg’s importance for the Danish king grew during the 18th century up until the Danish defeat in the Second 
Schleswig War in 1864, the Mint was only functional for four years during the reign of Frederik IV .

schleswig_holstein.meta_line: DENMARK. Lauenburg. 2/3 Taler (Zweidrittel), 1830-FA/FF.
schleswig_holstein.body_excerpt:
DENMARK. Lauenburg. 2/3 Taler (Zweidrittel), 1830-FA/FF. 
Altona Mint. Frederik VI. NGC MS-61. KM-25; Hede-1; Sieg-
44; Schou-3; Bruun-8082. Mintmaster: Johan Friedrich Freund. 
Engraver: Frederik Alsing. Although minted for use in Lauen -
burg (Lauenborg) this type was struck in Altona in a number of 
only 4,051 pieces. Excellent Mint State example of the only Dan-
ish emission from the small duchy on the borders of Schleswig-

The mintmaster is unknown and hides behind the enigmatic, in-
cuse hourglass mintmark. 
€6,000-€8,000
From the L.E. Bruun Collection.

schleswig_holstein.meta_line: GERMANY . Schleswig-Holstein-Gottorp. 2 Mark, ND (1681-
schleswig_holstein.body_excerpt:
GERMANY . Schleswig-Holstein-Gottorp. 2 Mark, ND (1681-
82). Schleswig Mint. Christian Albrecht. NGC EF-40. Dav-
unlisted (cf. 577); KM-unlisted (cf. 136); Sieg-16.1; Lange-399C; 
Bruun-14473. Weight: 22.00 gms. Mintmaster: Claus Jakob 
Mecklenburg. Excessive rare ND issue with the Duke’s Motto 
‘Per Aspera Ad Astra’ on the reverse instead of denomination 
and date. Excellent surfaces with old toning.
The famous Christian Lange Collection only had a rubbing of the 
example in the Danish National Museum and knew of one more 
example, sold in Copenhagen in 1871 as Lot #2612 at C. J. Thom-
sen’s

schleswig_holstein.meta_line: GERMANY . Schleswig-Holstein-Gottorp. 2 Mark, 1698. Tönning Mint. Friedrich IV . NGC EF-45. Dav-579; KM-
schleswig_holstein.body_excerpt:
GERMANY . Schleswig-Holstein-Gottorp. 2 Mark, 1698. Tönning Mint. Friedrich IV . NGC EF-45. Dav-579; KM-
166; Sieg-7; Lange-430AA; Bruun-14494. Weight: 22.00 gms. Mintmaster: Hans Heinrich Luders. Excessively rare sin-
gle-year emission; Mottled toning with a weakness to the edge that interferes with the legends on both sides.
The scarcity of this coin is illustrated not only by a lack of recorded sales on CoinArchives but also by the fact that Lange 
only had a rubbing of the example in the Danish National Museum.
€6,000-€8,000
From the L.E. Bruun Collection.
Ex: Countship of Brahesminde Coll

schleswig_holstein.meta_line: GERMANY . Schleswig-Holstein-Gottorp. Mark, 1698. Tön -
schleswig_holstein.body_excerpt:
