ning Mint. Friedrich IV . NGC EF-45. KM-165; Sieg-6; Lange-
430A; Bruun-14495. Weight: 10.84 gms. Mintmaster: Hans 
Heinrich Luders. Very rare single-year emission. Excellent de-
tails with a slight weakness as often seen on the coins from that 
mint, and an old, uneven toning.
Mark emissions from Schleswig-Holstein are rare, this being the 
second of only two. 
€2,000-€2,400
From the L.E. Bruun Collection.
Ex: Countship of Brahesminde Collection (Private Sale - 1922).

schleswig_holstein.meta_line: GERMANY . Schleswig-Holstein-Sonderburg. Taler,
schleswig_holstein.body_excerpt:
GERMANY . Schleswig-Holstein-Sonderburg. Taler, 
1627//1626. Reinfeldt or Sonderburg Mint. Alexander. NGC 
VF Details—Cleaned. Dav-3717; KM-28; Sieg-5; Lange-560; 
Bruun-14678. Weight: 27.26 gms. Mintmaster: Johann Lilien-
thal. Very rare Taler struck on the death of Alexander in 1627. 
Unevenly toned but gleaming with multicolor. An interesting 
zwitter/mule in that the reverse die of the 1626 Taler was reused 
on this coin, giving it two different dates.
The Norwegian lion sits atop the reverse arms as the dukes bore the 
hereditary title of ‘heir to Norway’ or ‘Heres Norvegie’. 
€3,000-€4,0

portrait, the coin has been repatinating for over a century and 
a stunning, metallic-blue color is spreading from the periphery 
on both sides. The reverse seems practically untouched and the 
charming shield topped with helmets stands razor-sharp.

schleswig_holstein.meta_line: GERMANY . Schleswig-Holstein-Schaumburg-Pinneberg.
schleswig_holstein.body_excerpt:
GERMANY . Schleswig-Holstein-Schaumburg-Pinneberg. 
1/2 Taler, ND (1601-22). Altona Mint. Ernst III. NGC AU-53. 
KM-102; Lange cf. 920; Weinmeister-159; Bruun-14918. Weight: 
14.27 gms. Extremely rare Half Taler in extraordinary condition. 
An old, dark patina covers the surfaces and gives the coin, along 
with the colorful toning, a most sublime expression. Light wear 
to the high points but well-struck and with excellent details.
Portrait coins from Ernst III are all very rare; the sublime portrait 
and spectacular condition of this coin makes it a true unicorn. 
€1,000-€1,400
From the L.E.

schleswig_holstein.meta_line: DENMARK. Speciedaler, 1646/5. Glückstadt Mint. Christian IV . NGC MS-60. Dav-3670; KM-27; Hede-163C; Sieg-186.3;
schleswig_holstein.body_excerpt:

Harck-38.4.3; Schou-35; Lange-40b; Bruun-5801. Weight: 28.75 gms. Mintmaster: Jacob Schwieger (IS). Engraver: signed (IL). A most 
pleasing piece of this ever popular type that is rarely encountered in MS condition.
The nude Roman goddess of fortune and personification of luck, Fortuna, standing on a globe and waving a banner, was chosen by Chris-
tian IV as a metaphor when the city of Glückstadt was founded in 1616.

schleswig_holstein.meta_line: DENMARK. 4 Rigsbankskilling, 1842. Altona Mint. Christian VIII. NGC MS-66. KM-721.3; Hede-8C; Sieg-8.3; Schou-6; Bru -
schleswig_holstein.body_excerpt:
DENMARK. 4 Rigsbankskilling, 1842. Altona Mint. Christian VIII. NGC MS-66. KM-721.3; Hede-8C; Sieg-8.3; Schou-6; Bru -
un-8152. Mintmaster: Johan Friedrich Freund (FF). Engraver: Frederik Christopher Krohn (FK). NGC TOP POP . Extremely rare 
variation struck in Altona in the Duchies. 
While more than one million were minted of this type in 1842, only a handful are known to have survived. The majority were not put into 
circulation but kept as a reserve in Altona. When the city fell to the rebels in 1848, the coins were seized and melted down. 
€3,000-€5,000
From the L. E. Bruun Collection.

schleswig_holstein.meta_line: DENMARK. 2 Frederik d’Or, 1853. Altona Mint. Frederik VII. NGC MS-64. KM-750.2; Hede-1B; Sieg-16.2; Schou-1; Bruun-8216.
schleswig_holstein.body_excerpt:

Weight: 13.27 gms. Mintmaster: Johan Friedrich Freund (FF). Engraver: Frederik Christopher Krohn (FK). NGC TOP POP . Rare, one-
year type with silky surfaces, a few small bagmarks.
The Christian and Frederik d’Or coinage The first minting of d’Or coinage in Denmark took place in 1771 and 1775 but it is believed to be 
a coinage for a single specific use the Duchies. 1826 saw a few Double d’Ors minted, but from 1827 minting took place on a larger scale. The 
coins, imitating

OUT.body_excerpt:

highly sought after when they do. It is doubly rare when they are preserved anywhere near this nicely, with 
this example surpassing all others on the NGC census. The surfaces are bright and lustrous, with only

OUT.meta_line: NORW AY . Mark, 1545. Gimsøy Mint. Christian III. NGC EF Details—Cleaned. Hede-2; Sieg-3; NMD-
OUT.body_excerpt:
NORW AY . Mark, 1545. Gimsøy Mint. Christian III. NGC EF Details—Cleaned. Hede-2; Sieg-3; NMD-
5; Schou-6; Bruun-9300. Weight: 9.55 gms. Mintmaster: Jørgen Kock, the younger. A few  minor flan flaws 
don’t detract from the fact that it is a lovely example, clearly struck and with almost no wear. Extremely rare, 
with only three are thought to exist in private collections, including another example in the L. E. Bruun 
collection, which is struck from different dies (Schou 7). The one not in the Bruun collection is holed, further 
underscoring the beauty of this example, both relative and real.

OUT.meta_line: NORW AY . Sølvgylden (Gimsøydaler), 1546. Gimsøy Mint. Christian III. NGC AU Details—Cleaned.
OUT.body_excerpt:

considered the piece de resistance of any collection of Norwegian coins, can hardly be overestimated. Of

Pedersen was appointed mintmaster on the 28th of April. This and all the following Specie-denominations

24; Sieg-32; NMD-12; Schou-2; Aagaard-47.1; Bruun-9711. Weight: 115.10 gms. Mintmaster: Frederik 
Grüner (FG). A truly monumental numismatic treasure, highly detailed, with plenty of luster. Along the rims 
is lovely toning alive with patches of blue and gold. The small imperfections in the obverse fields are due to 
the heftiness of the planchet, as can be seen in the additional photo of the edge. Of the six known Quadruple 
Speciedalers from Frederik III, this is the only one in private hands.

OUT.meta_line: SWEDEN. Riksdaler, 1534. Stockholm Mint. Gustav Vasa. NGC VF-30. MB-48; Dav-8692; SM-106(99);
OUT.body_excerpt:
SWEDEN. Riksdaler, 1534. Stockholm Mint. Gustav Vasa. NGC VF-30. MB-48; Dav-8692; SM-106(99); 
Delzanno-7: Hagander-3; Appelgren-479;  Bruun auction-370; Bruun-11443. Weight: 28.88 gms. “Crown” 
variation. This is the first Taler of Sweden, struck in the style and size of the Joachimstalers by the counts of 
Schlick. It also marks the transformation of Swedish coinage from medieval coinage. The enigmatic mintmark 
on the reverse, resembling an ear, is yet to be attributed to a mintmaster. Few pieces have survived to this 
day; most were probably melted down to produce Talers of inferior silver

OUT.meta_line: SWEDEN. 3 Riksdaler, ND (1587). Stockholm Mint. Johan III. NGC AU-53. Dav-LS570; SM-18(18);

OUT.body_excerpt:
SWEDEN. Riksdaler, 1597. Stockholm Mint. Karl (IX), Duke of Sodermanland. NGC AU-55.  Dav-
8712; SM-14(14); Delzanno-10: Hagander-45 (die match); Bruun auction-651; Bruun-11682. Weight: 28.83 
gms. This Riksdaler is the only denomination struck in Stockholm by Karl IX as duke, and all his coins are 
rare today. It is the first Swedish coin to have supporters for the shield, a feature that became standard on 
Riksdalers from 1654 to 1748; we do note the lion tucking his tail between his legs, an intriguing feature. The 
surfaces display a warm appeal, with nice strike quality and soft amber hue

good luster, with sharp details throughout. Only a light touch of wear is seen on the elevated portions of the 
design, with limited strike weakness. Some minor disturbances on the edge are also noted for completeness. 
A popular issue, due to the crossover appeal between Sweden, Poland, an

Engraver: Arvid Karlsteen. “Gripenhjelms Ducat” , struck by regional governor Boran Nils Gripenhielm, 
commemorates the discovery of gold at the Östra Silverberget in Dalarna 1695. This is the first time the origin 
of the gold is mentioned on a Swedish coin. Only a handful are known to exist outside of public collections.

alluring eye appeal and charm, this nearly-Choice Ducat demonstrates a brilliant essence of soft luster that

variation of Christian’s Stockholm coinag

OUT.body_excerpt:
SWEDEN. Daler, 1550. Svartsjö Mint. Gustav Vasa. NGC AU-55. Dav-8698; SM-170a(163); Delzanno-29; Bruun 
Auction-379; Bruun-11490. Weight: 28.92 gms. Mintmaster: Hans Hansson. Engraver: Erik Olufsson. Among the most 
beautiful of the 16th century’s Swedish coins, with a both delicate and powerful portrait of the king on the obverse and 
of Christ on the reverse. That, and the very high numeric grade for type will surely make for fierce bidding, as this coin 
will be a jewel in any collection.
The Svartsjö Mint was only operational from 1540 to 1550.
€4,000-€5,000
From the L. E. Bruun Collection

OUT.body_excerpt:

OUT.body_excerpt:

NMH-56; Josefsen-p. 98; Bruun-9021; Weight: 0.45 gms. A simply incredible survivor from one of the rarest and most 
sought-after of all Norwegian kings. With its amazing luster and lovely toning, this piece needs to be seen to be believed.
The provenance of this historically very interesting coin is second to non. It formed part of the only coin hoard ever found on 
the Faroe Island. The treasure that consisted of 98 silver coins buried between 1070 and 1080 was found in 1863 and pub-
lishe

OUT.body_excerpt:

NM-16 (1976); NM-1 (1992); NMH-115; Bruun-9058; Weight: 0.46 gms. An exquisite iridescent toning further en -
hances the considerable eye-appeal of this marvelous Medieval Penny struck with fully readable legends. The two-sided 
Pennies of Sverre Sigurdsson which closely imitates the Short Cross coinage of Henry II belong among the fabled rari-
ties of Norwegian numismatic history with very few in private ownership.
Most Norwegian 12th century coins are “mute” with the present type being a r

OUT.meta_line: NORW AY . 1/4 Penny (?), ND (1177-1202). Bergen Mint (?). Sverre Sigurdsson. NGC AU-53. Schive-VI:90; NM-18:1
OUT.body_excerpt:
NORW AY . 1/4 Penny (?), ND (1177-1202). Bergen Mint (?). Sverre Sigurdsson. NGC AU-53. Schive-VI:90; NM-18:1 
(1976); NM-8 (1992); Bruun-9063; Weight: 0.06 gms. Hairline split in flan but nicely centered, toned and with excellent 
detail. 
Almost all of the Bracteates minted in the latter part of the 12th century were of the so-called “lettered” type, and the few ex-
ceptions, like the present with a crowned, facing head are extremely rare. This type seems to have struck over a longer period 
of time with varying weights, and the denomination is uncertain. 
€2,500-€3,500
From the L. E. Bruun

(1976); Simensen-Fig. 73; Bruun-9098; Weight: 0.14 gms. A slight chip at the edge removes nothing from the fact that 
this is an absolutely stunning near Gem example with a lovely toning. Exceptionally rare, and one of the only Bracteates 
that carries a proper legend, R E X.

standard of the Norwegian monetary issues to a reasonable level, 
and at the time this type was known as an “Eirik Gross”. Outright 
warfare with the Danes and constant feuding with the Catholic 
Church (earning him the nickname “Priesthater”) however soon 
debased the currency again.

OUT.meta_line: NORW AY . Penny, ND (1285-90). Oslo Mint. Håkon (V) Magnusson as Duke. NGC EF-40. Schive-X:5; NM-32:1
OUT.body_excerpt:
NORW AY . Penny, ND (1285-90). Oslo Mint. Håkon (V) Magnusson as Duke. NGC EF-40. Schive-X:5; NM-32:1 
(1976); NM-1 (1992); NMH-240; Bruun-9186; Weight: 1.37 gms. A very rare coin with an exquisite, dark patina and 
sharp details.
Håkon, king Eirik’s younger brother, ruled areas around Oslo and Stavanger as a duke from 1280 to 1299, with the right to 
issue coins of his own. After taking over the throne in 1299, Oslo gradually took over the functions of capital from Bergen. 
On this especially interesting variant, Haakon is wearing a Ducal crown in the shape of a wreath of roses.
€3,000-€4,000

OUT.body_excerpt:
NORW AY . Penny, ND (1299-1300). Oslo Mint (?). Håkon V Magnusson. NGC AU-55. Schive-X:23; NM-36 (1976); 
NM-1 (1992); NMH-246; Bruun-9196; Weight: 0.73 gms. Rare and exceptionally well-struck with unusually pleasing 
surfaces.
This type which was formerly believed to be a 1/2 Penny is known through the archives as a “ Albi Rosati” (White Roseate) 
that although of a lighter weight was struck with an increased fineness as a counterpart to the vehemently unpopular so-
called “Black Crown” Pennies. The design was copied from the Scottish Sterlings.
€4,000-€5,000
From the L. E. Bruun Collection.

OUT.meta_line: NORW AY . Penny, ND (1380-87). Oslo Mint (?). Olav IV Håkonsson. NGC AU-50. Schive-XII:51; NM-51 (1976);
OUT.body_excerpt:
NORW AY . Penny, ND (1380-87). Oslo Mint (?). Olav IV Håkonsson. NGC AU-50. Schive-XII:51; NM-51 (1976); 
NM-1 (1992); NMH-277; Bruun-9217; Weight: 0.20 gms. Old numbering in ink at lower obverse. Rare and of excep-
tional condition for type.
This highly interesting bracteate type with a crowned O, was struck for the underaged Olav by his mother, Queen Regent 
Margrethe I. No coins are known to have been struck for Olav (Oluf II) in Denmark. Olav IV was the last Norwegian king 
to be born on Norwegian soil until Harald V who took over the throne in 1991.
€2,500-€3,500
From the L. E. Bruun Coll

OUT.meta_line: SWEDEN. Penny, ND (1022-50). Sigtuna Mint. Anund Jakob. NGC AU Details—Peck Marked. Llt-7; Delzanno-104;
OUT.body_excerpt:
SWEDEN. Penny, ND (1022-50). Sigtuna Mint. Anund Jakob. NGC AU Details—Peck Marked. Llt-7; Delzanno-104; 
Malmer-14.310.1252; SMH-612; Bruun-11151. Weight: 1.43 gms. Moneyer: Thormodt. An old, dark toning covers the 
coin and boosts the already clear and well-struck devices. A very rare type with fully legible legends. The coin imitates 
Cnut’s Pointed Helmet coinage which is conventionally dated to the years between 1024 and 1030.
Of the two almost identical reverse dies from the moneyer Thormoth, Malmer knew of 28 coins (14 of each), with eight in 
private collections.
Son of Olof Skötkonung

OUT.body_excerpt:

der-53; Bruun-11703. Weight: 4.96 gms. Mintmaster: Antoni Grooth the Elder. Extremely rare and delightfully lustrous 
coin with a clear strike and a most excellent portrait. The very light bend can hardly be seen on the reverse and the 
overall condition makes this coin a contender for the best in private hands.

worth of goods imported, 10 Rikdaler and 5 Hungarian Gulde

OUT.body_excerpt:

of Östergötland. NGC AU Details—Cleaned. Dav-4514; KM-
81.2; SM-1a; Delzanno-9; Hagander-63; Bruun-11743. Weight: 
31.37 gms. Mintmaster: David Kohl. Very rare two-year emis-
sion; hints of luster in the legend and only very light circulation 
wear with the reverse especially beautiful. 
A discreet ink number can be seen in obverse field, ‘2619’. The 
number refers to “Madai’s Vollsttändiges Thaler-Cabinet” from 
1767, which was used as a reference well into the 19th century.
Johan, prince of Östergötland, was the son of King Johan III,

ation is the even rarer with the 'REFVG' spelling on the obverse.

with a planchet flaw by the rim.

OUT.meta_line: SWEDEN. Salvator Riksdaler, 1631. Sala Mint. Gustav II Ad-
OUT.body_excerpt:
SWEDEN. Salvator Riksdaler, 1631. Sala Mint. Gustav II Ad-
olf. NGC EF Details—Cleaned. Dav-4520; KM-143; SM-36b; 
Delzanno-24; Hagander-cf. 81; Bruun-11850. Weight: 28.87 
gms. Mintmaster: Markus Kock. Very rare and excellently toned 
example; the concavity of the strike means the obverse appears 
worn but that is for the most part in the strike; the reverse is 
lustrous and beautiful.
The minting of silver coins stopped in 1626 in Stockholm and was 
resumed in Sala in 1631. In 1632 the mint moved back to Stock -
holm, but all silver coins dated 1631 are struck in Sala.

OUT.body_excerpt:

II Adolf. NGC AU Details—Bent. Fr-112f; KM-68; SB-3b; SG-
29; Hagander-89; Bruun-11871. Weight: 3.44 gms. Mintmaster: 
Johann Bartholomäus Hohleisen. Rare variation with horseshoe 
mintmark. Light waviness with no stress marks to the metal, 
hints of luster playing in the legend and a sharp strike with very 
little wear.
Augsburg in Southern Bavaria was conquered by Swedish forces in

burg coins in the name of Gustav II Adolf were minted in 1632,

OUT.meta_line: SWEDEN. Erfurt. Ducat, 1634. Erfurt Mint. Gustav II Adolf.
OUT.body_excerpt:

in the very central parts of the reverse; a homogenous appear -
ance and hardly any wear but lots of eye-appeal.
These posthumous strikes (Gustav II Adolf died in 1632) have been 
subject to intense studies (Ottar Ertzheid Collection in particular). 
This specific variation does not appear to be included, though. It

OUT.body_excerpt:

notice the feathers on the eagles and the central medallion on the

OUT.body_excerpt:

Riga was the second largest city in the Swedish Empire, surpassed only by the capital of Stockholm, and was held by the 
Swedes from the conquest by Gustav II Adolf (Gustavus Adolphus) in 1621 till the fall to the Russians during the plague of 
1710 (and The Great Northern War).

OUT.body_excerpt:

XII. NGC EF-40. Dav-1872; KM-X,M1; SB-221b; Bruun-12653. 
Weight: 29.06 gms. Mintmaster: Johan Memmies. Light wear 
but with handsome grey surfaces and luster; multicolor toning 
around the devices.
Before the Battle of Poltava in July 1709, which Karl XII famously 
lost, he had invaded Saxony to force Augustus II to renounce the 
Polish throne, which he did. Karl then restored the rights of reli -
gious freedom to the Protestants there. This Taler was struck com-
memorating these restored rights. 
€1,600-€1,800
From the L.E. Bruun Collec

OUT.body_excerpt:

OUT.body_excerpt:

most coveted Scandinavian coin of all. Tingström knew of 24 exam

OUT.body_excerpt:

no-152; Tingström-A1; Bruun-13818. Dimensions: 244 x 21mm. Weight: 3057 gms. Very rare; Tingström records 23

city changed from Catholic to Protestant hands several time during the Thirty Years’ War and would at the Westphalian 
Peace in 1648 be recognized as bi-confessional Lutheran and Catholic. While this is a German issue we retain the Bruun

GERMANY . Schleswig-Holstein-Gottorp. Lübeck (Bishopric). Taler, 1606-MP . Lübeck Mint. Johann Adolf. NGC 
AU Details—Cleaned. Dav-5438; KM-26.1; Lange-268a cf.; Sieg-9.9; Bruun-14116. Weight: 28.83 gms. Rare. Variation 
with ‘Co OL ‧ E ‧ DE ‧’ . Mintmaster: Matthias Puls. Minted by Johann Adolf as Bishop of Lübeck. Appealing and pre-
sentable, with limited wear and much charm despite the cleaning.
€2,500-€3,000
From the L. E. Bruun Collection.
Ex: Leopold Welzl v. Wellenheim Collection (Vienna - 1847) Lot # 5135.

lubeck_bishopric.meta_line: GERMANY . Schleswig-Holstein-Gottorp. Lübeck (Bishopric). Taler, 1683/78-HR. Eutin Mint. August Friedrich.
lubeck_bishopric.body_excerpt:
GERMANY . Schleswig-Holstein-Gottorp. Lübeck (Bishopric). Taler, 1683/78-HR. Eutin Mint. August Friedrich. 
NGC MS-62. Dav-5442; KM-63; Lange-504c; Bruun-15028. Weight: 29.56 gms. Mintmaster: Hans Ridder. Extremely 
rare Taler in excellent condition and of the highest historical importance to match its Mint State grade. The number 
‘3325’ written ink on the obverse refers to its number in ‘Madai’s Thaler-Cabinett’ from 1765.
Listed in Bruun’s catalogue as 1688 but appears to be 1683 over 1678.
€3,000-€5,000
From the L. E. Bruun Collection.
Ex: Ludwig de Traux Collection (Vienna - 1857), Lot3 4

lubeck_bishopric.meta_line: GERMANY . Schleswig-Holstein-Gottorp. Lübeck (Bishopric). Ducat, 1724. Eutin Mint. Christian August. NGC
lubeck_bishopric.body_excerpt:
GERMANY . Schleswig-Holstein-Gottorp. Lübeck (Bishopric). Ducat, 1724. Eutin Mint. Christian August. NGC 
MS-62. Fr-1508; KM- 80.2; Lange-510AAb; Bruun-15044. Weight: 3.45 gms. Mintmaster: Johan Justus Jaster. Engraver: 
Ehrenreich Hannibal. Excessively rare Ducat, only 50 struck, and missing from the auction circuit for decades if not 
longer. Even Lange only had a galvanic copy in his collection. A magnificent looking piece with full luster and lots of 
eye-appeal, this coin is the perfect combination of beauty, rarity and conservation.
€15,000-€25,000
From the L. E. Bruun Collection.
Ex: Fr

lubeck_bishopric.meta_line: GERMANY . Schleswig-Holstein-Gottorp. Lübeck (Bishopric). Taler, 1724. Eutin Mint. Christian August. NGC
lubeck_bishopric.body_excerpt:
GERMANY . Schleswig-Holstein-Gottorp. Lübeck (Bishopric). Taler, 1724. Eutin Mint. Christian August. NGC 
MS-62. Dav-2409; KM-79; Lange-511; Bruun-15046. Weight: 29.22 gms. Mintmaster: Johann Justus Jaster. Engraver: 
Ehrenreich Hannibal. A great rarity in incredible condition.
The Younger brother of Friedrich IV of Schleswig-Holstein-Gottorp, Christian Albert was given the small duchy of Eutin 
and was elected Bishop of Lübeck in 1706. He served as co-ruler in Schleswig-Holstein-Gottorp from the death of his brother 
until the coming-of-age of his nephew, Karl Friedrich, in 1717.
€8,000-€10,0

lubeck_bishopric.meta_line: GERMANY . Oldenburg. Lübeck (Bishopric). 5 Taler (Pistole - Friederich Augustd’ or), 1776-B. Altona Mint. Fried-
lubeck_bishopric.body_excerpt:
GERMANY . Oldenburg. Lübeck (Bishopric). 5 Taler (Pistole - Friederich Augustd’ or), 1776-B. Altona Mint. Fried-
rich August. NGC MS-61. Fr-1509 and 1931; KM-87 (Lübeck); Trippler/Kalvelage-369; Lange-522; Bruun-15142. 
Weight: 6.66 gms. Mintmaster: Casper Heinrich Lyng. Engraver: Johann Ephraim Bauert. Very rare, only 580 struck; 
200 in 1776 and a further 380 in 1781 using the same dies, making any distinction impossible. Superb condition, the 
second highest ranking on the NGC census.
Struck by Friedrich August as both Duke of Oldenburg and Bishop of Lübeck. His coinage can therefore be fou

GERMANY . Lübeck (Bishopric). Taler, 1612. Bremervörde 
Mint. Johann Friedrich. NGC EF-40. Dav-5073; KM-19.1; 
Lange-cf. 492C; Bruun-14975. Weight: 28.49 gms. Extremely 
rare date and type. Lovely, dark toning with hints of gold and 
multicolor and only slight wear to the high points. Some marks 
in the obverse fields. 
€2,000-€3,000
From the L.E. Bruun Collection.
Ex: Countship of Brahesminde Collection (Private Sale - 1922).
Ex: Ole Devegge Collection (Copenhagen - 1851, 1866, 1867) Lot # 
5548.

GERMANY . Lübeck (Bishopric). 2 Mark, 1723-AW . Glück -
stadt Mint. Christian August. NGC AU-53. Dav-623; KM-78.1; 
Lange-511A; Bruun-15039. Weight: 17.43 gms. Mintmaster: An-
dreas Woltereck. A very rare 2 Mark or Gulden as seen by the 
lack of a photo or even a drawing of it in Davenport. The surfaces 
are uncleaned with a greenish tint hiding promises of luster, and 
while there is a softness to the top points in the portrait it seems 
to be a weakness in the strike rather than wear or possibly a com-
bination.
Christian August of Holstein-Gottorp-Eutin was the grandson on 
his mother’s sid

GERMANY . Lübeck (Bishopric). 2 Mark, 1723-AW . Glück -
stadt Mint. Christian August. NGC MS-62. Dav-623; KM-78.2; 
Lange-cf. 511A; Bruun-15040. Weight: 17.10 gms. Mintmaster: 
Andreas Woltereck. A very rare 2 Mark or Gulden in extraordi-
nary condition; old, greenish tinted toning covers the uncircu -
lated surfaces with luster shimmering underneath. A die crack 
runs through the portrait. The elegant details in the hair shows 
off not only the artistry of the die cutter but also the condition of 
the piece, as there are no flattened part, neither from weak strike 
nor wear.
No recorded sales

lubeck_bishopric.meta_line: GERMANY . Oldenburg. Lübeck (Bishopric). Taler, 1775. Altona Mint. Friedrich August. NGC AU-58. Dav-2411;
lubeck_bishopric.body_excerpt:
GERMANY . Oldenburg. Lübeck (Bishopric). Taler, 1775. Altona Mint. Friedrich August. NGC AU-58. Dav-2411; 
KM-85; Trippler/Kalvelage-371; Lange 523; Bruun-15140. Weight: 28.89 gms. Mintmaster: Caspar Heinrich Lyng. Excel-
lent condition for type with playful luster around the devices and attractive, old toning; only 1,000 pieces struck.
Struck to Danish standard in 1777 re-using the reverse of the Albertstaler of 1775, the obverse bears the title ‘DVX REGN 
OLD’ - ‘Duke Regent of Oldenburg’, a title he was only granted in 1776.
Friedrich August only had three coins struck: the 1776 gold Pistol

oldenburg.meta_line: GERMANY . Oldenburg. Taler, ND (1538). Anton. NGC EF-40. Dav-9606; Trippler/Kalvelage-119; Bruun-15088.
oldenburg.body_excerpt:
GERMANY . Oldenburg. Taler, ND (1538). Anton. NGC EF-40. Dav-9606; Trippler/Kalvelage-119; Bruun-15088. 
Weight: 28.41 gms. Extremely rare and the first Taler from Oldenburg. Trippler/Kalvelage reports 9 (not including this). 
Immensely pleasing with an antique feel from the deep toning that melds perfectly with the consistent wear.
€15,000-€18,000
From the L. E. Bruun Collection.

oldenburg.body_excerpt:
